Motivation: The paper aims to address the inefficiency of dense multilayer architectures in spiking neural networks by exploring single-neuron models inspired by autapses.

Method: TDA-SNN employs a single leaky integrate-and-fire neuron and a prototype-training strategy, which reorganizes internal temporal states to mimic complex architectures like MLPs and convolutional networks.

Result: Experimental results on diverse benchmarks show the model achieves competitive performance with reduced computational resources and trades off with increased temporal latency.

Conclusion: TDA-SNN demonstrates the feasibility of temporally multiplexed single-neuron models for compact and efficient neuromorphic computing.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are promising for neuromorphic computing, but high-performing models still rely on dense multilayer architectures with substantial communication and state-storage costs. Inspired by autapses, we propose time-delayed autapse SNN (TDA-SNN), a framework that reconstructs SNNs with a single leaky integrate-and-fire neuron and a prototype-learning-based training strategy. By reorganizing internal temporal states, TDA-SNN can realize reservoir, multilayer perceptron, and convolution-like spiking architectures within a unified framework. Experiments on sequential, event-based, and image benchmarks show competitive performance in reservoir and MLP settings, while convolutional results reveal a clear space--time trade-off. Compared with standard SNNs, TDA-SNN greatly reduces neuron count and state memory while increasing per-neuron information capacity, at the cost of additional temporal latency in extreme single-neuron settings. These findings highlight the potential of temporally multiplexed single-neuron models as compact computational units for brain-inspired computing.

</details>


### [326] [Associative Memory using Attribute-Specific Neuron Groups-2: Learning and Sequential Associative Recall between Cue Neurons for different Cue Balls](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24910)
*Hiroshi Inazawa*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: This paper proposes a neural network model that learns and recalls associative memory across multiple attributes using QR code image patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to enhance associative memory models to handle realistic complex and diverse data with meaningful connections, despite the experimental absence of clear relations among attributes.

Method: Five attribute-processing systems (color, shape, size, scenery names, constellation names) are implemented using CB-RN systems trained with QR code image patterns for associative memory recall.

Result: When an image pattern is presented to one CB-RN system, the model generates a sequential chain recall of related attribute patterns across all trained systems.

Conclusion: The model demonstrates the capability to simulate associative memory recall across multiple distinct attribute-processing systems using neural networks.

Abstract: This paper introduces a neural network model that learns multiple attributes as images and performs associated, sequential recall of the learned memories. Briefly, the model presented here is an associative memory model that extends previous models [1] by increasing the number of attributes. In the real world, memory recall generates a chain of associations consisting of complex and diverse data with meaningful relations. However, because this experimental system is designed to implement and verify the processing operations behind such operations, we believe it is not a problem if the associative memory (i.e., the chain of data) is composed of attributes that do not necessarily have clear relation with each other. Accordingly, the attribute-processing systems prepared in this study consist of five types: the C.CB-RN system for processing color attributes, the S.CB-RN system for shape attributes, and the V.CB-RN system for size attributes, as adopted in our previous paper [1], as well as the SV.CB-RN system for processing the names of the world's most beautiful scenery (spectacular view names) and the CN.CB-RN system for processing constellation names. As before, the data presented to each CB-RN system are represented as image patterns using QR codes [2]. These five types of CB-RN systems will be combined and trained with QR code pattern images of the attribute elements of each system. After that, when a pattern image of an attribute element is presented to any of the CB-RN systems, a mechanism will be constructed in which a chain (associative) recall of pattern images of related attribute elements in the other trained systems will be generated.

</details>


### [327] [NERO-Net: A Neuroevolutionary Approach for the Design of Adversarially Robust CNNs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25517)
*Inês Valentim,Nuno Antunes,Nuno Lourenço*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper introduces NERO-Net, a neuroevolutionary approach for designing CNNs with intrinsic robustness to adversarial attacks, demonstrating improved performance on CIFAR-10.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of attention on designing neural network architectures that are intrinsically robust to adversarial attacks, particularly for safety-critical applications.

Method: The proposed method involves using neuroevolution to design architectures while isolating architectural robustness through a fitness function that avoids adversarial training but promotes post-attack accuracy.

Result: The fittest architecture achieved 33% robustness accuracy against FGSM during the evolutionary search, 47% after further standard training, with clean accuracies of 87% and 93%, respectively. Adversarial training improved robustness against AutoAttack to 40%.

Conclusion: NERO-Net demonstrates that neuroevolution can yield CNN architectures with intrinsic adversarial robustness, validated on CIFAR-10, offering a new direction for robust model design.

Abstract: Neuroevolution automates the complex task of neural network design but often ignores the inherent adversarial fragility of evolved models which is a barrier to adoption in safety-critical scenarios. While robust training methods have received significant attention, the design of architectures exhibiting intrinsic robustness remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we propose NERO-Net, a neuroevolutionary approach to design convolutional neural networks better equipped to resist adversarial attacks. Our search strategy isolates architectural influence on robustness by avoiding adversarial training during the evolutionary loop. As such, our fitness function promotes candidates that, even trained with standard (non-robust) methods, achieve high post-attack accuracy without sacrificing the accuracy on clean samples. We assess NERO-Net on CIFAR-10 with a specific focus on $L_\infty$-robustness. In particular, the fittest individual emerged from evolutionary search with 33% accuracy against FGSM, used as an efficient estimator for robustness during the search phase, while maintaining 87% clean accuracy. Further standard training of this individual boosted these metrics to 47% adversarial and 93% clean accuracy, suggesting inherent architectural robustness. Adversarial training brings the overall accuracy of the model up to 40% against AutoAttack.

</details>


<div id='cs.PL'></div>

# cs.PL [[Back]](#toc)

### [328] [Model2Kernel: Model-Aware Symbolic Execution For Safe CUDA Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24595)
*Mengting He,Shihao Xia,Haomin Jia,Wenfei Wu,Linhai Song*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: Model2Kernel is presented as a system that verifies memory safety for CUDA kernels in LLM inference, addressing issues like memory-safety bugs caused by complex tensor layouts and parallelism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory-safety bugs in CUDA kernels used in LLM inference can lead to severe consequences such as corrupted model weights, service crashes, or adversarial exploitations, making this a critical problem to address.

Method: Model2Kernel employs model-aware dynamic analysis to classify kernel arguments, followed by CUDA-specialized symbolic execution with new abstractions for dynamic tensor memory and thread identifiers.

Result: Model2Kernel identified 353 previously unknown bugs in tested systems, with only nine false positive detections, showcasing its practical effectiveness.

Conclusion: Model2Kernel represents a significant advancement in verifying memory safety in CUDA kernels for LLM inference systems, offering reliable and scalable solutions for modern computing infrastructure.

Abstract: The widespread adoption of large language models (LLMs) has made GPU-accelerated inference a critical part of modern computing infrastructure. Production inference systems rely on CUDA kernels to implement core transformer operations, yet these kernels are highly susceptible to memory-safety bugs due to model-dependent tensor layouts, intricate memory indexing, and massive thread-level parallelism. Such bugs can corrupt model weights, crash inference services, or even enable adversarial attacks. Existing techniques either depend on unavailable hardware, incur high overhead, or fail to handle kernel inputs with variable lengths, and none can effectively detect CUDA memory bugs in LLM inference systems. This paper presents Model2Kernel, the first practical system for automatically verifying the memory safety of CUDA kernels used in LLM inference. Model2Kernel performs model-aware dynamic analysis to determine how each model invokes kernels and to classify kernel arguments as either fixed by the model architecture or controlled by model users. Using this information, Model2Kernel then applies CUDA-specialized symbolic execution, supported by new abstractions for dynamic tensor memory and thread identifiers, to accurately pinpoint memory bugs in kernels. In the evaluation on CUDA kernels and models from vLLM, Hugging Face, and recent LLM research papers, Model2Kernel discovers 353 previously unknown bugs while producing only nine false positives, demonstrating its effectiveness.

</details>


### [329] [ReSyn: A Generalized Recursive Regular Expression Synthesis Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24624)
*Seongmin Kim,Hyunjoon Cheon,Su-Hyeon Kim,Yo-Sub Han,Sang-Ki Ko*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: The paper presents ReSyn, a divide-and-conquer framework, and Set2Regex, a novel regex synthesizer, to tackle complex real-world regex synthesis challenges effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PBE systems underperform on complex, real-world regex benchmarks due to high structural complexity like deeper nesting and frequent unions.

Method: The authors propose ReSyn, a framework that divides regex synthesis tasks into smaller problems, and Set2Regex, a synthesizer that efficiently models the permutation invariance of input examples.

Result: Experimental data shows ReSyn improves accuracy across various synthesizers significantly. When combined with Set2Regex, it achieves state-of-the-art performance on tough, real-world benchmarks.

Conclusion: ReSyn, as a framework, and Set2Regex, as a synthesizer, provide an effective solution to real-world regex synthesis problems, setting a new performance standard in the field.

Abstract: Existing Programming-By-Example (PBE) systems often rely on simplified benchmarks that fail to capture the high structural complexity-such as deeper nesting and frequent Unions-of real-world regexes. To overcome the resulting performance drop, we propose ReSyn, a synthesizer-agnostic divide-and-conquer framework that decomposes complex synthesis problems into manageable sub-problems. We also introduce Set2Regex, a parameter-efficient synthesizer capturing the permutation invariance of examples. Experimental results demonstrate that ReSyn significantly boosts accuracy across various synthesizers, and its combination with Set2Regex establishes a new state-of-the-art on challenging real-world benchmark.

</details>


### [330] [Numerical Superoptimization for Library Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24812)
*Jonas Regehr,Mitch Briles,Zachary Tatlock,Pavel Panchekha*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: The paper focuses on creating efficient numerical libraries by identifying and implementing new mathematical primitives that improve the performance of floating-point operations in scientific applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The current landscape of numerical software optimization heavily relies on existing mathematical primitives. However, the question of discovering and implementing new primitives that could further enhance performance and accuracy has not been systematically addressed.

Method: The authors introduce GrowLibm, a system that repurposes the Herbie superoptimizer to identify, rank, and prune potential new primitives that would increase workload performance. It uses search procedures, equivalency checks, and cost modeling techniques inherent in superoptimizers to evaluate the utility of new primitives.

Result: GrowLibm was evaluated on three scientific applications (PROJ, CoolProp, Basilisk) and identified primitives that significantly improved kernel speed by up to 2.2x and accuracy from 56.0% to 93.5%. An LLVM-based matcher further improved application performance by detecting and utilizing the learned primitives.

Conclusion: GrowLibm demonstrates that leveraging the capabilities of numerical superoptimizers can lead to the discovery of impactful mathematical primitives, offering substantial performance and accuracy gains in numerical software.

Abstract: Numerical software depends on fast, accurate implementations of mathematical primitives like sin, exp, and log. Modern superoptimizers can optimize floating-point kernels against a given set of such primitives, but a more fundamental question remains open: which new primitives are worth implementing in the first place?
  We formulate this as numerical library learning: given a workload of floating-point kernels, identify the mathematical primitives whose expert implementations would most improve speed and accuracy. Our key insight is that numerical superoptimizers already have the machinery well-suited to this problem. Their search procedures happen to enumerate candidate primitives, their equivalence procedures can generalize and deduplicate candidates, and their cost models can estimate counterfactual utility: how much the workload would improve if a given primitive were available.
  We present GrowLibm, which repurposes the Herbie superoptimizer as a numerical library learner. GrowLibm mines candidate primitives from the superoptimizer's intermediate search results, ranks them by counterfactual utility, and prunes redundant candidates. Across three scientific applications (PROJ, CoolProp, and Basilisk), GrowLibm identifies compact, reusable primitives that can be implemented effectively using standard numerical techniques. When Herbie is extended with these expert implementations, kernel speed improves by up to 2.2x at fixed accuracy, and maximum achievable accuracy also improves, in one case from 56.0% to 93.5%. We also prototype an LLVM matcher that recognizes learned primitives in optimized IR, recovering 26 replacement sites across five PROJ projections and improving end-to-end application performance by up to 5%.

</details>


### [331] [Evaluating adaptive and generative AI-based feedback and recommendations in a knowledge-graph-integrated programming learning system](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24940)
*Lalita Na Nongkhai,Jingyun Wang,Adam Wynn,Takahiko Mendori*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: The paper proposes integrating a large language model (LLM) with a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) strategy into an adaptive learning system to provide code feedback. A hybrid approach combining GenAI and adaptive feedback yielded the best results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve formative feedback for learners' code submissions and recommend exercises effectively by leveraging advanced AI techniques.

Method: An experimental study using a framework combining a knowledge graph, user interaction history, and a large language model integrated with RAG. The study compares adaptive, GenAI, and hybrid GenAI-adaptive feedback approaches using log analysis from 4956 submissions.

Result: Learners using GenAI-assisted modes produced more correct code and fewer submissions with missing logic compared to the adaptive-only mode. The hybrid approach performed the best, yielding the highest correct submissions and lowest incomplete attempts.

Conclusion: Hybrid GenAI-adaptive instructional mode is more effective in enhancing learner performance and is positively perceived for usability, usefulness, and feedback quality.

Abstract: This paper introduces the design and development of a framework that integrates a large language model (LLM) with a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approach leveraging both a knowledge graph and user interaction history. The framework is incorporated into a previously developed adaptive learning support system to assess learners' code, generate formative feedback, and recommend exercises. Moerover, this study examines learner preferences across three instructional modes; adaptive, Generative AI (GenAI), and hybrid GenAI-adaptive. An experimental study was conducted to compare the learning performance and perception of the learners, and the effectiveness of these three modes using four key log features derived from 4956 code submissions across all experimental groups. The analysis results show that learners receiving feedback from GenAI modes had significantly more correct code and fewer code submissions missing essential programming logic than those receiving feedback from adaptive mode. In particular, the hybrid GenAI-adaptive mode achieved the highest number of correct submissions and the fewest incorrect or incomplete attempts, outperforming both the adaptive-only and GenAI-only modes. Questionnaire responses further indicated that GenAI-generated feedback was widely perceived as helpful, while all modes were rated positively for ease of use and usefulness. These results suggest that the hybrid GenAI-adaptive mode outperforms the other two modes across all measured log features.

</details>


### [332] [On Representability of Multiple-Valued Functions by Linear Lambda Terms Typed with Second-order Polymorphic Type System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25337)
*Satoshi Matsuoka*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates two styles of representing multiple-valued functions in linear lambda terms within a polymorphic type framework, along with optimizations and applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to explore efficient representations and applications of multiple-valued functions using lambda terms within polymorphic type systems.

Method: Two representation styles were investigated: a circuit style mimicking combinational circuits and an inductive style following mathematical traditions. Optimizations were also proposed.

Result: The paper successfully represents multiple-valued functions using both styles and identifies optimizations, demonstrating the applicability of these methods in various domains.

Conclusion: Both representation styles provide viable methods for encoding multiple-valued functions, with practical optimizations and applications highlighted in the case study.

Abstract: We show that any multiple-valued function can be represented by a linear lambda term typed in a second-order polymorphic type system, using two distinct styles. The first is a circuit style, which mimics combinational circuits in switching theory. The second is an inductive style, which follows a more traditional mathematical approach. We also discuss several optimizations for these representations. Furthermore, we present a case study that demonstrates the potential applications of our approach across various domains.

</details>


### [333] [Decidable By Construction: Design-Time Verification for Trustworthy AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25414)
*Houston Haynes*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework to ensure AI model correctness at design time by verifying properties related to stability and consistency upfront, without post hoc enforcement, leveraging algebraic constraints over abelian groups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to eliminate the need for costly post hoc enforcement of AI model correctness, especially for high-stakes applications, by introducing a method that verifies these properties during the design phase.

Method: The framework uses algebraic structures based on finitely generated abelian groups to enforce correctness. It integrates results like a dimensional type system, program hypergraphs, and adaptive domain models to verify and preserve stability, correctness, and consistency.

Result: The framework simplifies the process of ensuring AI reliability by enabling Hindley-Milner unification over abelian groups, which computes hypotheses using a computable restriction of Solomonoff's universal prior.

Conclusion: The proposed framework reduces computational overhead and improves reliability by ensuring correctness upfront, providing a more efficient alternative to existing post hoc methods in AI systems.

Abstract: A prevailing assumption in machine learning is that model correctness must be enforced after the fact. We observe that the properties determining whether an AI model is numerically stable, computationally correct, or consistent with a physical domain do not necessarily demand post hoc enforcement. They can be verified at design time, before training begins, at marginal computational cost, with particular relevance to models deployed in high-leverage decision support and scientifically constrained settings. These properties share a specific algebraic structure: they are expressible as constraints over finitely generated abelian groups $\mathbb{Z}^n$, where inference is decidable in polynomial time and the principal type is unique. A framework built on this observation composes three prior results (arXiv:2603.16437, arXiv:2603.17627, arXiv:2603.18104): a dimensional type system carrying arbitrary annotations as persistent codata through model elaboration; a program hypergraph that infers Clifford algebra grade and derives geometric product sparsity from type signatures alone; and an adaptive domain model architecture preserving both invariants through training via forward-mode coeffect analysis and exact posit accumulation. We believe this composition yields a novel information-theoretic result: Hindley-Milner unification over abelian groups computes the maximum a posteriori hypothesis under a computable restriction of Solomonoff's universal prior, placing the framework's type inference on the same formal ground as universal induction. We compare four contemporary approaches to AI reliability and show that each imposes overhead that can compound across deployments, layers, and inference requests. This framework eliminates that overhead by construction.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [334] [Saranga: MilliWatt Ultrasound for Navigation in Visually Degraded Environments on Palm-Sized Aerial Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24699)
*Manoj Velmurugan,Phillip Brush,Colin Balfour,Richard J. Przybyla,Nitin J. Sanket*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Tiny, agile drones often struggle with visual navigation due to limited payload and visibility challenges. This study introduces "Saranga," a low-power, ultrasound-based navigation system inspired by bats to combat these limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing navigation systems for small aerial robots are inadequate in GPS-denied or visually degraded environments like fog or darkness due to limitations in current sensors (e.g., cameras, LIDAR, RADAR).

Method: The researchers implemented a dual sonar array alongside two noise-combatting techniques: propeller noise blocking and a deep learning denoising model trained with synthetic and real-world noise data.

Result: "Saranga" enables palm-sized drones to effectively navigate in visually compromised conditions through ultrasound-based sensing, demonstrated successfully in fog, darkness, and other challenging environments.

Conclusion: This study shows the effectiveness of a low-power, ultrasound-based navigation system in overcoming previously insurmountable visual challenges for small drones.

Abstract: Tiny palm-sized aerial robots possess exceptional agility and cost-effectiveness in navigating confined and cluttered environments. However, their limited payload capacity directly constrains the sensing suite on-board the robot, thereby limiting critical navigational tasks in Global Positioning System (GPS)-denied wild scenes. Common methods for obstacle avoidance use cameras and LIght Detection And Ranging (LIDAR), which become ineffective in visually degraded conditions such as low visibility, dust, fog or darkness. Other sensors, such as RAdio Detection And Ranging (RADAR), have high power consumption, making them unsuitable for tiny aerial robots. Inspired by bats, we propose Saranga, a low-power ultrasound-based perception stack that localizes obstacles using a dual sonar array. We present two key solutions to combat the low Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio of $-4.9$ decibels: physical noise reduction and a deep learning based denoising method. Firstly, we present a practical way to block propeller induced ultrasound noise on the weak echoes. The second solution is to train a neural network to utilize the \textcolor{black}{long horizon of ultrasound echoes} for finding signal patterns under high amounts of uncorrelated noise where classical methods were insufficient. We generalize to the real world by using a synthetic data generation pipeline and limited real noise data for training. We enable a palm-sized aerial robot to navigate in visually degraded conditions of dense fog, darkness, and snow in a cluttered environment with thin and transparent obstacles using only on-board sensing and computation. We provide extensive real world results to demonstrate the efficacy of our approach.

</details>


### [335] [FODMP: Fast One-Step Diffusion of Movement Primitives Generation for Time-Dependent Robot Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24806)
*Xirui Shi,Arya Ebrahimi,Yi Hu,Jun Jin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FODMP introduces a fast trajectory prediction model for robots, improving real-time control and success rates in various benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion models for robot learning face trade-offs between speed and capturing complex motion dynamics, limiting their real-time applicability.

Method: Introduces FODMP, a one-step ProDMP-based distillation framework that retains temporal movement structure while drastically enhancing inference speed.

Result: FODMP outperforms existing methods in terms of speed by up to 10x while maintaining or improving success rates in manipulation tasks and enables new capabilities like intercepting fast-moving objects.

Conclusion: FODMP resolves key limitations of preceding methods, offering a balance of efficiency and robustness, making it suitable for real-time robotic control and new motion primitive capabilities.

Abstract: Diffusion models are increasingly used for robot learning, but current designs face a clear trade-off. Action-chunking diffusion policies like ManiCM are fast to run, yet they only predict short segments of motion. This makes them reactive, but unable to capture time-dependent motion primitives, such as following a spring-damper-like behavior with built-in dynamic profiles of acceleration and deceleration. Recently, Movement Primitive Diffusion (MPD) partially addresses this limitation by parameterizing full trajectories using Probabilistic Dynamic Movement Primitives (ProDMPs), thereby enabling the generation of temporally structured motions. Nevertheless, MPD integrates the motion decoder directly into a multi-step diffusion process, resulting in prohibitively high inference latency that limits its applicability in real-time control settings. We propose FODMP (Fast One-step Diffusion of Movement Primitives), a new framework that distills diffusion models into the ProDMPs trajectory parameter space and generates motion using a single-step decoder. FODMP retains the temporal structure of movement primitives while eliminating the inference bottleneck through single-step consistency distillation. This enables robots to execute time-dependent primitives at high inference speed, suitable for closed-loop vision-based control. On standard manipulation benchmarks (MetaWorld, ManiSkill), FODMP runs up to 10 times faster than MPD and 7 times faster than action-chunking diffusion policies, while matching or exceeding their success rates. Beyond speed, by generating fast acceleration-deceleration motion primitives, FODMP allows the robot to intercept and securely catch a fast-flying ball, whereas action-chunking diffusion policy and MPD respond too slowly for real-time interception.

</details>


### [336] [A Nonvolatile Switchable-polarity EPM Valve](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24811)
*Bingchao Wang,Jonah Mack,Francesco Giorgio-Serchi,Adam A. Stokes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a new bistable magnetic architecture, S-EPM, that advances scalable control of pneumatic and fluidic networks by eliminating the need for continuous power and enabling innovative logic-embedded fluidic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of existing pneumatic and fluidic control networks that require continuous power, external hardware, and fixed routing, which impede scalability and efficiency.

Method: Develop and validate the Switchable-polarity ElectroPermanent Magnet (S-EPM), capable of bistable magnetic states without sustained power, and integrate it into a pinch-valve to enable logic-embedded fluidic networks with novel routing capabilities.

Result: Demonstration of a state-encoded magnetic control system that achieves programmable, nonvolatile fluidic routing architectures, including decoders and six-port configurations, with enhanced scalability and efficiency.

Conclusion: The S-EPM technology establishes a scalable and energy-efficient approach for digital fluidics, enabling autonomous laboratory platforms and addressing challenges in traditional fluidic systems.

Abstract: Scalable control of pneumatic and fluidic networks remains fundamentally constrained by architectures that require continuous power input, dense external control hardware, and fixed routing topologies. Current valve arrays rely on such continuous actuation and mechanically fixed routing, imposing substantial thermal and architectural overhead. Here, we introduce the Switchable-polarity ElectroPermanent Magnet (S-EPM), a fundamentally new bistable magnetic architecture that deterministically reverses its external magnetic polarity through transient electrical excitation. By reconfiguring internal flux pathways within a composite magnet assembly, the S-EPM establishes two stable, opposing magnetic configurations without requiring sustained power. We integrate this architecture into a compact pinch-valve to robustly control pneumatic and liquid media. This state-encoded magnetic control enables logic-embedded fluidic networks, including decoders, hierarchical distribution modules, and a nonvolatile six-port routing array. These systems provide address-based routing and programmable compositional control, offering features like individual port isolation that are impossible with standard mechanically coupled rotary valves. By embedding functionality in persistent magnetic states rather than continuous power or static plumbing, this work establishes a scalable foundation for digital fluidics and autonomous laboratory platforms.

</details>


### [337] [Characterization of Constraints in Flexible Unknown Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24813)
*Samrat Bhattacharyya,Nabil Simaan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces an online path planning algorithm for robots to safely manipulate flexibly constrained objects in unknown environments by identifying and characterizing constraints in real-time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robots to autonomously manipulate objects safely in unknown environments without pre-existing system knowledge, particularly for applications like surgical organ retraction.

Method: The authors developed methods to identify flexible constraints and stiffness in real-time, allowing robots to explore and manipulate objects using local force and position data. Global stiffness behavior is determined via an atlas of mechanical constraints.

Result: The algorithm was validated through simulations and experiments, showcasing real-time recognition of mechanical constraints and identification of screw parameters for safe manipulation.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates the feasibility of simultaneous stiffness exploration and manipulation, paving the way for applications in areas like surgical robotics requiring safe cooperative manipulation.

Abstract: This paper presents an online path planning algorithm for safe autonomous manipulation of a flexibly constrained object in an unknown environment. Methods for real time identification and characterization of perceived flexible constraints and global stiffness are presented. Used in tandem, these methods allow a robot to simultaneously explore, characterize, and manipulate an elastic system safely. Navigation without a-priori knowledge of the system is achieved using constraint exploration based on local force and position information. The perceived constraint stiffness is considered at multiple poses along an object's (system) trajectory. Using stiffness eigenvector information, global stiffness behavior is characterized and identified using an atlas of simple mechanical constraints, such as hinges and planar constraints. Validation of these algorithms is carried out by simulation and experimentally. The ability to recognize several common simple mechanical constraints (such as a flexible hinge) in real time, and to subsequently identify relevant screw parameters is demonstrated. These results suggest the feasibility of simultaneous global constrain/stiffness exploration and safe manipulation of flexibly constrained objects. We believe that this approach will eventually enable safe cooperative manipulation in applications such as organ retraction and manipulation during surgery

</details>


### [338] [Integrated Multi-Drone Task Allocation, Sequencing, and Optimal Trajectory Generation in Obstacle-Rich 3D Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24908)
*Yunes Alqudsi,Murat Makaraci*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents IMD-TAPP, an integrated framework for allocating tasks and planning trajectories for drone teams in cluttered 3D environments, ensuring safety and dynamic feasibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of coordinating aerial robots in obstacle-rich 3D spaces by integrating task allocation and trajectory planning into a unified framework.

Method: IMD-TAPP uses a 3D navigation graph for obstacle-aware cost computation, an Injected Particle Swarm Optimization scheme for assignment and ordering, and a minimum-snap trajectory generation algorithm with iterative safety validation.

Result: The proposed framework consistently generates dynamically feasible, collision-free trajectories and achieves competitive mission completion times, exemplified by a case study achieving a minimum mission time of 136s for two drones.

Conclusion: IMD-TAPP is effective in integrating task allocation and trajectory planning for drone teams, maintaining safety and efficiency in challenging environments.

Abstract: Coordinating teams of aerial robots in cluttered three-dimensional (3D) environments requires a principled integration of discrete mission planning-deciding which robot serves which goals and in what order -- with continuous-time trajectory synthesis that enforces collision avoidance and dynamic feasibility. This paper introduces IMD-TAPP (Integrated Multi-Drone Task Allocation and Path Planning), an end-to-end framework that jointly addresses multi-goal allocation, tour sequencing, and safe trajectory generation for quadrotor teams operating in obstacle-rich spaces. IMD--TAPP first discretizes the workspace into a 3D navigation graph and computes obstacle-aware robot-to-goal and goal-to-goal travel costs via graph-search-based pathfinding. These costs are then embedded within an Injected Particle Swarm Optimization (IPSO) scheme, guided by multiple linear assignment, to efficiently explore coupled assignment/ordering alternatives and to minimize mission makespan. Finally, the resulting waypoint tours are transformed into time-parameterized minimum-snap trajectories through a generation-and-optimization routine equipped with iterative validation of obstacle clearance and inter-robot separation, triggering re-planning when safety margins are violated. Extensive MATLAB simulations across cluttered 3D scenarios demonstrate that IMD--TAPP consistently produces dynamically feasible, collision-free trajectories while achieving competitive completion times. In a representative case study with two drones serving multiple goals, the proposed approach attains a minimum mission time of 136~s while maintaining the required safety constraints throughout execution.

</details>


### [339] [CROSS: A Mixture-of-Experts Reinforcement Learning Framework for Generalizable Large-Scale Traffic Signal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24930)
*Xibei Chen,Yifeng Zhang,Yuxiang Xiao,Mingfeng Fan,Maonan Wang,Guillaume Sartoretti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CROSS introduces a decentralized RL framework using Mixture-of-Experts to tackle challenges in Adaptive Traffic Signal Control by identifying latent traffic patterns and enabling scenario-specific strategies, outperforming traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL-based ATSC methods struggle to generalize across diverse traffic scenarios due to limitations in representational capacity and adaptability.

Method: The paper proposes a two-module framework: Predictive Contrastive Clustering (PCC) for latent traffic pattern identification and Scenario-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) for scenario-specific strategies.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets using SUMO simulator show CROSS outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in performance and generalization.

Conclusion: CROSS successfully addresses large-scale ATSC challenges, improving traffic management through enhanced representation and adaptation to diverse scenarios.

Abstract: Recent advances in robotics, automation, and artificial intelligence have enabled urban traffic systems to operate with increasing autonomy towards future smart cities, powered in part by the development of adaptive traffic signal control (ATSC), which dynamically optimizes signal phases to mitigate congestion and optimize traffic. However, achieving effective and generalizable large-scale ATSC remains a significant challenge due to the diverse intersection topologies and highly dynamic, complex traffic demand patterns across the network. Existing RL-based methods typically use a single shared policy for all scenarios, whose limited representational capacity makes it difficult to capture diverse traffic dynamics and generalize to unseen environments. To address these challenges, we propose CROSS, a novel Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)-based decentralized RL framework for generalizable ATSC. We first introduce a Predictive Contrastive Clustering (PCC) module that forecasts short-term state transitions to identify latent traffic patterns, followed by clustering and contrastive learning to enhance pattern-level representation. We further design a Scenario-Adaptive MoE module that augments a shared policy with multiple experts, thus enabling adaptive specialization and more flexible scenario-specific strategies. We conduct extensive experiments in the SUMO simulator on both synthetic and real-world traffic datasets. Compared with state-of-the-art baselines, CROSS achieves superior performance and generalization through improved representation of diverse traffic scenarios.

</details>


### [340] [COIN: Collaborative Interaction-Aware Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Self-Driving Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24931)
*Yifeng Zhang,Jieming Chen,Tingguang Zhou,Tanishq Duhan,Jianghong Dong,Yuhong Cao,Guillaume Sartoretti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces the COIN framework which leverages MARL for improving collaboration and efficiency in multi-agent self-driving systems, outperforming baselines in dense and dynamic traffic scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address challenges in achieving efficient and safe collaboration among autonomous vehicles in dynamic multi-agent self-driving systems, especially in dense traffic scenarios.

Method: COIN uses a novel CIG-TD3 algorithm with a centralized training, decentralized execution setup, optimizing both individual (navigation) and global (collaboration) objectives. It includes a dual-level interaction-aware centralized critic for better value estimation and credit assignment.

Result: Simulation experiments show COIN consistently improves both safety and efficiency, outperforming other advanced baselines in various MASD system sizes. This was confirmed through real-world robot demonstrations.

Conclusion: The COIN framework is highly effective in handling complex MASD scenarios, making it a superior solution for next-generation intelligent transportation systems.

Abstract: Multi-Agent Self-Driving (MASD) systems provide an effective solution for coordinating autonomous vehicles to reduce congestion and enhance both safety and operational efficiency in future intelligent transportation systems. Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) has emerged as a promising approach for developing advanced end-to-end MASD systems. However, achieving efficient and safe collaboration in dynamic MASD systems remains a significant challenge in dense scenarios with complex agent interactions. To address this challenge, we propose a novel collaborative(CO-) interaction-aware(-IN) MARL framework, named COIN. Specifically, we develop a new counterfactual individual-global twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient (CIG-TD3) algorithm, crafted in a "centralized training, decentralized execution" (CTDE) manner, which aims to jointly optimize the individual objectives (navigation) and the global objectives (collaboration) of agents. We further introduce a dual-level interaction-aware centralized critic architecture that captures both local pairwise interactions and global system-level dependencies, enabling more accurate global value estimation and improved credit assignment for collaborative policy learning. We conduct extensive simulation experiments in dense urban traffic environments, which demonstrate that COIN consistently outperforms other advanced baseline methods in both safety and efficiency across various system sizes. These results highlight its superiority in complex and dynamic MASD scenarios, as further validated through real-world robot demonstrations. Supplementary videos are available at https://marmotlab.github.io/COIN/

</details>


### [341] [SABER: A Stealthy Agentic Black-Box Attack Framework for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24935)
*Xiyang Wu,Guangyao Shi,Qingzi Wang,Zongxia Li,Amrit Singh Bedi,Dinesh Manocha*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces SABER, a system designed to create minimal adversarial textual edits that significantly impair vision-language-action (VLA) models' performance in robotic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While VLA models integrate vision, language, and action for natural-language-guided robotic tasks, they are vulnerable to small adversarial changes in instructions, necessitating robust evaluation methods.

Method: SABER employs a GRPO-trained ReAct attacker to make adversarial textual edits (using character, token, and prompt-level tools) under a bounded edit budget, targeting behavior degradation in robotic tasks.

Result: SABER significantly reduces task performance metrics across six state-of-the-art VLA models on the LIBERO benchmark, showing decreases in task success rates (-20.6%), increases in sequence length (+55%), and higher constraint violations (+33%).

Conclusion: Minimal adversarial instruction edits can substantially undermine robot performance in VLA models, and SABER provides a scalable, effective black-box method for testing and identifying these vulnerabilities.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models enable robots to follow natural-language instructions grounded in visual observations, but the instruction channel also introduces a critical vulnerability: small textual perturbations can alter downstream robot behavior. Systematic robustness evaluation therefore requires a black-box attacker that can generate minimal yet effective instruction edits across diverse VLA models. To this end, we present SABER, an agent-centric approach for automatically generating instruction-based adversarial attacks on VLA models under bounded edit budgets. SABER uses a GRPO-trained ReAct attacker to generate small, plausible adversarial instruction edits using character-, token-, and prompt-level tools under a bounded edit budget that induces targeted behavioral degradation, including task failure, unnecessarily long execution, and increased constraint violations. On the LIBERO benchmark across six state-of-the-art VLA models, SABER reduces task success by 20.6%, increases action-sequence length by 55%, and raises constraint violations by 33%, while requiring 21.1% fewer tool calls and 54.7% fewer character edits than strong GPT-based baselines. These results show that small, plausible instruction edits are sufficient to substantially degrade robot execution, and that an agentic black-box pipeline offers a practical, scalable, and adaptive approach for red-teaming robotic foundation models.

</details>


### [342] [Wireless bioelectronics for untethered biohybrid robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24959)
*Hiroyuki Tetsuka,Minoru Hirano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This article reviews recent advancements in control strategies for biohybrid robots, focusing on wireless electrical and optical stimulation and neuromuscular integration, while proposing the future direction of integrating neural organoids for autonomous systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the challenge of controlling biohybrid robots without tethered wiring or bulky hardware, aiming to enable wireless and more efficient stimulation and control mechanisms.

Method: The paper reviews recent technological advancements in wireless bioelectronics and optoelectronics and their integration into biohybrid robots to enhance remote control capabilities.

Result: The study identifies successful strategies for wireless control, such as electrical and optical stimulation and neuromuscular junction incorporation for selective actuator control.

Conclusion: The article concludes that future work should focus on integrating neural organoid-bioelectronics to create fully autonomous and closed-loop biohybrid robot systems.

Abstract: Biohybrid robots integrate living tissues with engineered artificial structures to achieve organism-inspired actuation and behavior. A persistent challenge is delivering stimulation and control signals without relying on tethered wiring or bulky hardware immersed in cell-culture media. Wireless bioelectronics addresses this limitation by enabling the remote transfer of control signals, typically via radio-frequency magnetic fields, to locally stimulate muscle tissues at tissue-electrode interfaces. In parallel, wireless optoelectronics enables remote control of optogenetically modified, muscle-based robots by embedding light emitters that initiate muscle actuation through light-gated ion channels. Further advances incorporate neuromuscular junctions, leveraging biological signal transduction to enable selective control of multiple actuators through wireless frequency- and time-division multiplexing. This perspective article summarizes recent advances in control strategies for biohybrid robots, namely, wireless electrical stimulation, wireless optical stimulation, and neuromuscular integration. Then this describes cross-cutting design principles and highlights a future direction, namely, co-integration of neural organoid-bioelectronics toward autonomous, closed-loop biohybrid robots.

</details>


### [343] [Learning Rollout from Sampling:An R1-Style Tokenized Traffic Simulation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24989)
*Ziyan Wang,Peng Chen,Ding Li,Chiwei Li,Qichao Zhang,Zhongpu Xia,Guizhen Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The authors introduce R1Sim, a tokenized traffic simulation policy that improves autonomous driving evaluation by utilizing reinforcement learning driven by motion token entropy patterns and achieving competitive performance against state-of-the-art models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing traffic simulation methods often rely on next-token prediction, limiting the exploration of valuable motion tokens in suboptimal regions. This paper aims to address this limitation by leveraging motion token entropy patterns for better exploration.

Method: The authors propose R1Sim, which incorporates an entropy-guided adaptive sampling mechanism to focus on high-uncertainty motion tokens, and optimizes behaviors using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) combined with a safety-aware reward design.

Result: R1Sim demonstrates a balanced trade-off between exploration and exploitation, resulting in realistic, safe, and diverse multi-agent traffic behaviors. It achieves competitive performance on the Waymo Sim Agent benchmark.

Conclusion: The introduction of entropy-based mechanisms in R1Sim marks a promising direction for improving the quality and diversity of traffic simulation. The experimental results validate its effectiveness in advancing autonomous driving evaluations.

Abstract: Learning diverse and high-fidelity traffic simulations from human driving demonstrations is crucial for autonomous driving evaluation. The recent next-token prediction (NTP) paradigm, widely adopted in large language models (LLMs), has been applied to traffic simulation and achieves iterative improvements via supervised fine-tuning (SFT). However, such methods limit active exploration of potentially valuable motion tokens, particularly in suboptimal regions. Entropy patterns provide a promising perspective for enabling exploration driven by motion token uncertainty. Motivated by this insight, we propose a novel tokenized traffic simulation policy, R1Sim, which represents an initial attempt to explore reinforcement learning based on motion token entropy patterns, and systematically analyzes the impact of different motion tokens on simulation outcomes. Specifically, we introduce an entropy-guided adaptive sampling mechanism that focuses on previously overlooked motion tokens with high uncertainty yet high potential. We further optimize motion behaviors using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), guided by a safety-aware reward design. Overall, these components enable a balanced exploration-exploitation trade-off through diverse high-uncertainty sampling and group-wise comparative estimation, resulting in realistic, safe, and diverse multi-agent behaviors. Extensive experiments on the Waymo Sim Agent benchmark demonstrate that R1Sim achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [344] [$π$, But Make It Fly: Physics-Guided Transfer of VLA Models to Aerial Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25038)
*Johnathan Tucker,Denis Liu,Aiden Swann,Allen Ren,Javier Yu,Jiankai Sun,Brandon Kim,Lachlain McGranahan,Quan Vuong,Mac Schwager*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents AirVLA, a system designed to adapt manipulation-pretrained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, originally made for fixed-base manipulators, to address aerial pick-and-place tasks through data augmentation and physics-informed guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models struggle to adapt to aerial platforms due to the distinct dynamic requirements of flight, creating a need for innovations in transferring such models to underactuated and dynamic aerial tasks.

Method: The authors introduced AirVLA, which includes a Payload-Aware Guidance mechanism to integrate payload constraints into the policy, combined with a Gaussian Splatting pipeline to synthesize navigation training data, enabling the system to better handle aerial dynamics without retraining the foundation model.

Result: The study conducted 460 real-world experiments showing a navigation success rate increase from 81% to 100% using synthesized data and a pick-and-place success rate improvement from 23% to 50% with Payload-Aware Guidance. For a long-horizon compositional task, the model achieved 62% overall success.

Conclusion: With proper data enhancements and physics-informed interventions, manipulation-pretrained VLAs can be effectively transferred to aerial tasks, demonstrating their potential for broader applicability beyond fixed-base systems.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models such as $π_0$ have demonstrated remarkable generalization across diverse fixed-base manipulators. However, transferring these foundation models to aerial platforms remains an open challenge due to the fundamental mismatch between the quasi-static dynamics of fixed-base arms and the underactuated, highly dynamic nature of flight. In this work, we introduce AirVLA, a system that investigates the transferability of manipulation-pretrained VLAs to aerial pick-and-place tasks. We find that while visual representations transfer effectively, the specific control dynamics required for flight do not. To bridge this "dynamics gap" without retraining the foundation model, we introduce a Payload-Aware Guidance mechanism that injects payload constraints directly into the policy's flow-matching sampling process. To overcome data scarcity, we further utilize a Gaussian Splatting pipeline to synthesize navigation training data. We evaluate our method through a cumulative 460 real-world experiments which demonstrate that this synthetic data is a key enabler of performance, unlocking 100% success in navigation tasks where directly fine-tuning on teleoperation data alone attains 81% success. Our inference-time intervention, Payload-Aware Guidance, increases real-world pick-and-place task success from 23% to 50%. Finally, we evaluate the model on a long-horizon compositional task, achieving a 62% overall success rate. These results suggest that pre-trained manipulation VLAs, with appropriate data augmentation and physics-informed guidance, can transfer to aerial manipulation and navigation, as well as the composition of these tasks.

</details>


### [345] [ThermoAct:Thermal-Aware Vision-Language-Action Models for Robotic Perception and Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25044)
*Young-Chae Son,Dae-Kwan Ko,Yoon-Ji Choi,Soo-Chul Lim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework that uses thermal data and a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to improve robot safety and task execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for human-robot collaboration primarily use visual data for decision-making, overlooking the potential benefits of integrating thermal information.

Method: The proposed Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework employs a Vision-Language Model to interpret natural language commands and incorporates thermal data to enhance safety and efficiency during robot task execution.

Result: Real-world experiments show that integrating thermal data improves task success and operational safety when compared to vision-only systems.

Conclusion: Thermal data integration through the VLA framework is a viable approach for improving safety and efficacy in human-robot collaboration environments.

Abstract: In recent human-robot collaboration environments, there is a growing focus on integrating diverse sensor data beyond visual information to enable safer and more intelligent task execution. Although thermal data can be crucial for enhancing robot safety and operational efficiency, its integration has been relatively overlooked in prior research. This paper proposes a novel Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that incorporates thermal information for robot task execution. The proposed system leverages a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a high-level planner to interpret complex natural language commands and decompose them into simpler sub-tasks. This approach facilitates efficient data collection and robust reasoning for complex operations. Unlike conventional methods that rely solely on visual data, our approach integrates thermal information, enabling the robot to perceive physical properties and proactively ensure environmental safety. Experimental results from real-world task scenarios validate the feasibility of our proposed framework, suggesting its potential to enhance task success rates and safety compared to existing vision-based systems.

</details>


### [346] [CTS-PLL: A Robust and Anytime Framework for Collaborative Task Sequencing and Multi-Agent Path Finding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25121)
*Junkai Jiang,Yitao Xu,Ruochen Li,Shaobing Xu,Jianqiang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CTS-PLL addresses the CTS-MAPF problem with hierarchical improvements, achieving higher success rates and quality in agent-based sequencing and pathfinding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research addresses the challenge of navigating and coordinating multiple agents to accomplish task sequences without collision, a problem made complex by its combinatorial nature.

Method: The paper introduces a hierarchical framework named CTS-PLL, incorporating a lock agent detection-release mechanism for local re-planning and a Large Neighborhood Search (LNS)-based refinement procedure for better robustness and solution quality.

Result: Extensive benchmarking shows CTS-PLL performs better in terms of success rates and solution quality compared to existing methods, alongside maintaining runtime efficiency. Real-world experiments confirm its practical feasibility.

Conclusion: CTS-PLL proves to be an effective solution for CTS-MAPF, offering substantial advantages in handling complex agent-based environments efficiently.

Abstract: The Collaborative Task Sequencing and Multi-Agent Path Finding (CTS-MAPF) problem requires agents to accomplish sequences of tasks while avoiding collisions, posing significant challenges due to its combinatorial complexity. This work introduces CTS-PLL, a hierarchical framework that extends the configuration-based CTS-MAPF planning paradigm with two key enhancements: a lock agents detection and release mechanism leveraging a complete planning method for local re-planning, and an anytime refinement procedure based on Large Neighborhood Search (LNS). These additions ensure robustness in dense environments and enable continuous improvement of solution quality. Extensive evaluations across sparse and dense benchmarks demonstrate that CTS-PLL achieves higher success rates and solution quality compared with existing methods, while maintaining competitive runtime efficiency. Real-world robot experiments further demonstrate the feasibility of the approach in practice.

</details>


### [347] [Dissimilarity-Based Persistent Coverage Control of Multi-Robot Systems for Improving Solar Irradiance Prediction Accuracy in Solar Thermal Power Plants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25139)
*Haruki Kawase,Taiga Sugawara,A. Daniel Carnerero*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new algorithm using dissimilarity mapping for solar irradiance forecasting that guides mobile sensors, demonstrating enhanced prediction accuracy through experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate solar irradiance prediction is crucial for efficient solar thermal power plant operations, but existing kriging-based methods lack dynamic sensor positioning for real-time optimization.

Method: A dissimilarity map derived from the kriging model is used in conjunction with a persistent coverage control algorithm to dynamically guide mobile sensors to areas needing additional data for better predictions.

Result: Experiments with mobile robots showed that the proposed method achieved more accurate solar irradiance predictions compared to baseline approaches under various simulated conditions.

Conclusion: Dynamic sensor positioning driven by the proposed algorithm improves prediction accuracy, validating its potential as a tool for optimizing solar irradiance forecasting.

Abstract: Accurate forecasting of future solar irradiance is essential for the effective control of solar thermal power plants. Although various kriging-based methods have been proposed to address the prediction problem, these methods typically do not provide an appropriate sampling strategy to dynamically position mobile sensors for optimizing prediction accuracy in real time, which is critical for achieving accurate forecasts with a minimal number of sensors. This paper introduces a dissimilarity map derived from a kriging model and proposes a persistent coverage control algorithm that effectively guides agents toward regions where additional observations are required to improve prediction performance. By means of experiments using mobile robots, the proposed approach was shown to obtain more accurate predictions than the considered baselines under various emulated irradiance fields.

</details>


### [348] [A Minimum-Energy Control Approach for Redundant Mobile Manipulators in Physical Human-Robot Interaction Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25259)
*Davide Tebaldi,Niccolò Paradisi,Fabio Pini,Luigi Biagiotti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a control method for mobile manipulators to minimize kinetic energy and optimize performance in human-robot interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the efficiency and safety of mobile manipulation systems in human-robot interactions, particularly in tasks unsuitable for fixed-base manipulators.

Method: A control approach minimizing the overall kinetic energy of a mobile manipulator, experimentally tested on a peg-in-hole task.

Result: The approach achieved reduced kinetic energy and improved performance compared to a benchmark method.

Conclusion: The proposed control methodology enhances system performance and energy efficiency for mobile manipulators in physical human-robot interactions.

Abstract: Research on mobile manipulation systems that physically interact with humans has expanded rapidly in recent years, opening the way to tasks which could not be performed using fixed-base manipulators. Within this context, developing suitable control methodologies is essential since mobile manipulators introduce additional degrees of freedom, making the design of control approaches more challenging and more prone to performance optimization. This paper proposes a control approach for a mobile manipulator, composed of a mobile base equipped with a robotic arm mounted on the top, with the objective of minimizing the overall kinetic energy stored in the whole-body mobile manipulator in physical human-robot interaction applications. The approach is experimentally tested with reference to a peg-in-hole task, and the results demonstrate that the proposed approach reduces the overall kinetic energy stored in the whole-body robotic system and improves the system performance compared with the benchmark method.

</details>


### [349] [Connectivity-Aware Representations for Constrained Motion Planning via Multi-Scale Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25298)
*Suhyun Jeon,Yumin Lim,Woo-Jeong Baek,Hyeonseo Kim,Suhan Park,Jaeheung Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method that learns a connectivity-aware representation to assist in constrained motion planning by choosing appropriate start and goal configurations, resulting in improved success rates and reduced planning times.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motion planning is often inefficient or infeasible in environments with disconnected regions or constraints that restrict feasible spaces, especially due to inverse kinematics redundancy. Existing approaches struggle with such challenges, necessitating a better way to select configurations for planning.

Method: The authors employ a multi-scale manifold learning approach to embed joint configurations into a latent space, then use clustering for pseudo-label generation. A contrastive learning framework is applied to ensure the selected configurations are connectivity-aware, improving the planning process.

Result: The method achieved 1.9 times higher success rates and reduced planning times by 57% (factor of 0.43) during experiments on various manipulation tasks, outperforming baseline methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses constraints and redundancy in motion planning by ensuring connectivity-aware configuration selection, leading to higher efficiency and effectiveness in planning tasks.

Abstract: The objective of constrained motion planning is to connect start and goal configurations while satisfying task-specific constraints. Motion planning becomes inefficient or infeasible when the configurations lie in disconnected regions, known as essentially mutually disconnected (EMD) components. Constraints further restrict feasible space to a lower-dimensional submanifold, while redundancy introduces additional complexity because a single end-effector pose admits infinitely many inverse kinematic solutions that may form discrete self-motion manifolds. This paper addresses these challenges by learning a connectivity-aware representation for selecting start and goal configurations prior to planning. Joint configurations are embedded into a latent space through multi-scale manifold learning across neighborhood ranges from local to global, and clustering generates pseudo-labels that supervise a contrastive learning framework. The proposed framework provides a connectivity-aware measure that biases the selection of start and goal configurations in connected regions, avoiding EMDs and yielding higher success rates with reduced planning time. Experiments on various manipulation tasks showed that our method achieves 1.9 times higher success rates and reduces the planning time by a factor of 0.43 compared to baselines.

</details>


### [350] [SafeGuard ASF: SR Agentic Humanoid Robot System for Autonomous Industrial Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25353)
*Thanh Nguyen Canh,Thang Tran Viet,Thanh Tuan Tran,Ben Wei Lim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces SafeGuard ASF, a framework that uses humanoid robots for hazard detection in dark factories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With the rise of unmanned dark factories, there is a growing need for autonomous safety systems capable of identifying and addressing diverse risks.

Method: The study develops SafeGuard ASF, leveraging RGB-D imaging, the ReAct reasoning framework, and locomotion policies trained with PPO on Unitree G1 robots.

Result: The system detects fire/smoke with 94.2% mAP and 127ms latency, supports enhanced locomotion, and performs tasks like patrol and human detection.

Conclusion: The framework effectively combines perception, reasoning, and actuation to enable humanoid robots to address diverse hazard scenarios in industrial settings.

Abstract: The rise of unmanned ``dark factories'' operating without human presence demands autonomous safety systems capable of detecting and responding to multiple hazard types. We present SafeGuard ASF (Agentic Security Fleet), a comprehensive framework deploying humanoid robots for autonomous hazard detection in industrial environments. Our system integrates multi-modal perception (RGB-D imaging), a ReAct-based agentic reasoning framework, and learned locomotion policies on the Unitree G1 humanoid platform. We address three critical hazard scenarios: fire and smoke detection, abnormal temperature monitoring in pipelines, and intruder detection in restricted zones. Our perception pipeline achieves 94.2% mAP for fire or smoke detection with 127ms latency. We train multiple locomotion policies, including dance motion tracking and velocity control, using Unitree RL Lab with PPO, demonstrating stable convergence within 80,000 training iterations. We validate our system in both simulation and real-world environments, demonstrating autonomous patrol, human detection with visual perception, and obstacle avoidance capabilities. The proposed ToolOrchestra action framework enables structured decision-making through perception, reasoning, and actuation tools.

</details>


### [351] [Bayesian Learning-Enhanced Navigation with Deep Smoothing for Inertial-Aided Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25364)
*Nadav Cohen,Itzik Klein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces BLENDS, a novel framework combining Bayesian learning and deep smoothing to enhance post-processing navigation accuracy, showing significant performance improvements on real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite fixed-interval smoothing being optimal under Gaussian assumptions, traditional INS/GNSS systems inherit biases from GNSS measurements, leading to persistent accuracy issues that existing methods cannot fully resolve.

Method: The BLENDS framework integrates a transformer-based neural network into the two-filter smoother, modifies covariance matrices, and directly corrects the smoothed error-state, utilizing a new Bayesian-consistent loss for supervision.

Result: BLENDS demonstrated up to 63% horizontal position accuracy improvement compared to the baseline forward EKF in tests on mobile robot and quadrotor datasets.

Conclusion: BLENDS successfully addresses systematic position bias in GNSS systems, providing a statistically consistent and significantly more accurate smoothing solution for navigation applications.

Abstract: Accurate post-processing navigation is essential for applications such as survey and mapping, where the full measurement history can be exploited to refine past state estimates. Fixed-interval smoothing algorithms represent the theoretically optimal solution under Gaussian assumptions. However, loosely coupled INS/GNSS systems fundamentally inherit the systematic position bias of raw GNSS measurements, leaving a persistent accuracy gap that model-based smoothers cannot resolve. To address this limitation, we propose BLENDS, which integrates Bayesian learning with deep smoothing to enhance navigation performance. BLENDS is a a data-driven post-processing framework that augments the classical two-filter smoother with a transformer-based neural network. It learns to modify the filter covariance matrices and apply an additive correction to the smoothed error-state directly within the Bayesian framework. A novel Bayesian-consistent loss jointly supervises the smoothed mean and covariance, enforcing minimum-variance estimates while maintaining statistical consistency. BLENDS is evaluated on two real-world datasets spanning a mobile robot and a quadrotor. Across all unseen test trajectories, BLENDS achieves horizontal position improvements of up to 63% over the baseline forward EKF.

</details>


### [352] [Integrating Deep RL and Bayesian Inference for ObjectNav in Mobile Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25366)
*João Castelo-Branco,José Santos-Victor,Alexandre Bernardino*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a hybrid framework combining Bayesian inference and deep reinforcement learning to improve autonomous object search in indoor environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges like partial observability, perceptual uncertainty, and efficient navigation during robotic object search.

Method: A hybrid framework integrating Bayesian belief maps with reinforcement learning for action selection. Tested in Habitat 3.0 simulation.

Result: Improved success rates and reduced search effort in indoor object-search tasks, validating the combination of Bayesian and learning-based techniques.

Conclusion: Combining probabilistic reasoning and adaptive policies enhances object search efficiency and reliability in complex indoor settings.

Abstract: Autonomous object search is challenging for mobile robots operating in indoor environments due to partial observability, perceptual uncertainty, and the need to trade off exploration and navigation efficiency. Classical probabilistic approaches explicitly represent uncertainty but typically rely on handcrafted action-selection heuristics, while deep reinforcement learning enables adaptive policies but often suffers from slow convergence and limited interpretability. This paper proposes a hybrid object-search framework that integrates Bayesian inference with deep reinforcement learning. The method maintains a spatial belief map over target locations, updated online through Bayesian inference from calibrated object detections, and trains a reinforcement learning policy to select navigation actions directly from this probabilistic representation. The approach is evaluated in realistic indoor simulation using Habitat 3.0 and compared against developed baseline strategies. Across two indoor environments, the proposed method improves success rate while reducing search effort. Overall, the results support the value of combining Bayesian belief estimation with learned action selection to achieve more efficient and reliable objectsearch behavior under partial observability.

</details>


### [353] [IntentReact: Guiding Reactive Object-Centric Navigation via Topological Intent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25382)
*Yanmei Jiao,Anpeng Lu,Wenhan Hu,Rong Xiong,Yue Wang,Huajin Tang,Wen-an Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study introduces IntentReact, a novel framework for object-goal visual navigation that bridges global topological planning and local object-centric control using intent-conditioned waypoint prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in object-centric navigation approaches, specifically the inability to incorporate global topological guidance into local control decisions, thereby improving navigation success and execution quality.

Method: IntentReact utilizes a low-dimensional directional signal termed "intent" that conditions the robot's waypoint prediction policy, enabling alignment with global topological guidance.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that IntentReact improves navigation success and execution quality compared to previous object-centric methods.

Conclusion: The framework enhances the robot's capability to reorient effectively and navigate more topologically consistent paths, boosting robustness while maintaining reactivity.

Abstract: Object-goal visual navigation requires robots to reason over semantic structure and act effectively under partial observability. Recent approaches based on object-level topological maps enable long-horizon navigation without dense geometric reconstruction, but their execution remains limited by the gap between global topological guidance and local perception-driven control. In particular, local decisions are made solely from the current egocentric observation, without access to information beyond the robot's field of view. As a result, the robot may persist along its current heading even when initially oriented away from the goal, moving toward directions that do not decrease the global topological distance. In this work, we propose IntentReact, an intent-conditioned object-centric navigation framework that introduces a compact interface between global topological planning and reactive object-centric control. Our approach encodes global topological guidance as a low-dimensional directional signal, termed intent, which conditions a learned waypoint prediction policy to bias navigation toward topologically consistent progression. This design enables the robot to promptly reorient when local observations are misleading, guiding motion toward directions that decrease global topological distance while preserving the reactivity and robustness of object-centric control. We evaluate the proposed framework through extensive experiments, demonstrating improved navigation success and execution quality compared to prior object-centric navigation methods.

</details>


### [354] [UMBRELLA: Uncertainty-aware Multi-robot Reactive Coordination under Dynamic Temporal Logic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25395)
*Qisheng Zhao,Meng Guo,Hengxuan Du,Lars Lindemann,Zhongkui Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents UMBRELLA, a framework for dynamic multi-robot coordination with moving targets, leveraging Conformal Prediction and Monte Carlo Tree Search for efficient planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most multi-robot systems assume static task environments or opt for simple replanning in dynamic settings, which lacks efficiency for collaborative tasks involving moving targets.

Method: The framework uses Conformal Prediction for motion uncertainty modeling, integrates Monte Carlo Tree Search with uncertainty-aware rollouts, and utilizes a CP-based metric to optimize Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR). Receding-horizon planning is employed for real-time task adjustment.

Result: Simulations and hardware experiments show that UMBRELLA reduces average makespan by 23% and its variance by 71% compared to static baseline methods.

Conclusion: UMBRELLA is a robust and efficient solution for dynamic task allocation in multi-robot systems, ensuring spatial-temporal constraint adherence and improving performance metrics significantly.

Abstract: Multi-robot systems can be extremely efficient for accomplishing team-wise tasks by acting concurrently and collaboratively. However, most existing methods either assume static task features or simply replan when environmental changes occur. This paper addresses the challenging problem of coordinating multi-robot systems for collaborative tasks involving dynamic and moving targets. We explicitly model the uncertainty in target motion prediction via Conformal Prediction(CP), while respecting the spatial-temporal constraints specified by Linear Temporal Logic (LTL). The proposed framework (UMBRELLA) combines the Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) over partial plans with uncertainty-aware rollouts, and introduces a CP-based metric to guide and accelerate the search.
