Our design is grounded in requirements derived from 20+ meetings, surveys, and interviews across a year-long industry partnership with Beko. Supporting features include Voice-to-Tour capture, comprehension quizzes, podcasts, and a dashboard. We deployed Lacy on Beko's production environment and conducted a controlled study on a legacy finance system (30K+ LOC). Learners using expert-guided tours achieved 83% quiz scores versus 57% for AI-only tours, preferred tours over traditional self-study, and reported they would need fewer expert consultations. Experts found tour creation less burdensome than live walkthroughs. Beko has since adopted Lacy for organizational onboarding, and we release our code and study instruments as a replication package.

</details>


### [385] [Quantum Circuit Repair by Gate Prioritisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25587)
*Eñaut Mendiluze Usandizaga,Thomas Laurent,Paolo Arcaini,Shaukat Ali*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: QRep is a system for automated repair of faulty quantum circuits, achieving a 70% full repair rate and efficient fault localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Repairing faulty quantum circuits is complex and lacks scalable, efficient automated solutions.

Method: QRep assigns suspiciousness scores to gates, iteratively applies patches, and prioritizes highly suspicious gates to localize and repair faults efficiently.

Result: QRep fully repaired 70% of evaluated circuits and localized faults effectively in the remaining cases, scaling up to circuits with 13 qubits.

Conclusion: QRep proves to be an effective and scalable method for automated fault repair in quantum circuits.

Abstract: Repairing faulty quantum circuits is challenging and requires automated solutions. We present QRep, an automated repair approach that iteratively identifies and repairs faults in a circuit. QRep uniformly applies patches across the circuit and assigns each gate a suspiciousness score, reflecting its likelihood of being faulty. It then narrows the search space by prioritising the most suspicious gates in subsequent iterations, increasing the repair efficiency. We evaluated QRep on 40 (real and synthetic) faulty circuits. QRep completely repaired 70% of them, and for the remaining circuits, the actual faulty gate was ranked within the top 44% most suspicious gates, demonstrating the effectiveness of QRep in fault localisation. Compared with two baseline approaches, QRep scales to larger and more complex circuits, up to 13 qubits.

</details>


### [386] [SHAPR: Operationalising Human-AI Collaborative Research Through Structured Knowledge Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25660)
*Ka Ching Chan*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: SHAPR is a framework combining human-centred and AI-assisted research practices, operationalised through structured cycles and modular knowledge units to enhance transparency, reproducibility, and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a structured approach for integrating human decisions and AI capabilities in research software development, ensuring transparent and scalable practices.

Method: The authors formalized SHAPR using models for research cycles, artefact evolution, and knowledge transformation, featuring Structured Knowledge Units and incorporating AI execution capabilities.

Result: SHAPR enables modular knowledge accumulation, systematic traceability, and adapts to varying levels of AI involvement, supporting scalable and transparent research workflows.

Conclusion: SHAPR provides a robust foundation for rigorous and reproducible AI-assisted research, enhancing methodological soundness and scalability.

Abstract: SHAPR (Solo Human-Centred and AI-Assisted Practice) is a framework for research software development that integrates human-centred decision-making with AI-assisted capabilities. While prior work introduced SHAPR as a conceptual framework, this paper focuses on its operationalisation as a structured, traceable, and knowledge-generating approach to AI-assisted research practice. We present a set of interconnected models describing how research activities are organised through iterative cycles (Explore-Build-Use-Evaluate-Learn), how artefacts evolve through development and use, and how empirical evidence is transformed into conceptual knowledge. Central to this process are Structured Knowledge Units (SKUs), which provide modular and reusable representations of insights derived from practice, supporting knowledge accumulation across cycles. The framework introduces evidence and traceability as a cross-cutting mechanism linking human decisions, AI-assisted development, and artefact evolution to enable transparency, reproducibility, and systematic refinement. SHAPR is also positioned as an AI-executable research framework, as its structured processes and documentation can be interpreted by generative AI systems to guide research workflows. Simultaneously, SHAPR supports a continuum of AI involvement, allowing researchers to balance control, learning, and automation across different contexts. Beyond individual workflows, SHAPR is conceptualised as an integrated research system combining LLM workspaces, development environments, cloud storage, and version control to support scalable, knowledge-centred research practices. Overall, SHAPR provides a practical and theoretically grounded foundation for conducting rigorous, transparent, and reproducible research in AI-assisted environments, contributing to the development of scalable and methodologically sound research practices.

</details>


### [387] [The Kitchen Loop: User-Spec-Driven Development for a Self-Evolving Codebase](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25697)
*Yannick Roy*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'Kitchen Loop,' a framework for autonomous, self-evolving software with a unified trust model and validates it through extensive experimentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current software bottlenecks revolve around knowing what to build and ensuring its trustworthiness rather than code production itself.

Method: The framework incorporates four components: specification surface, synthetic power testing with LLM, unbeatable tests for ground-truth verification, and drift control for quality measurement and pause gates.

Result: Validation across two production systems resulted in 285+ iterations, 1,094+ pull requests merged, and zero regressions detected, showcasing emergent properties like quality improvement and self-correction chains.

Conclusion: Although primitives aren't novel, their integration forms a safe, production-tested system for autonomous software evolution.

Abstract: Code production is now a commodity; the bottleneck is knowing what to build and proving it works. We present the Kitchen Loop, a framework for autonomous, self-evolving software built on a unified trust model: (1) a specification surface enumerating what the product claims to support; (2) 'As a User x 1000', where an LLM agent exercises that surface as a synthetic power user at 1,000x human cadence; (3) Unbeatable Tests, ground-truth verification the code author cannot fake; and (4) Drift Control, continuous quality measurement with automated pause gates. We validate across two production systems over 285+ iterations, producing 1,094+ merged pull requests with zero regressions detected by the regression oracle (methodology in Section 6.1). We observe emergent properties at scale: multi-iteration self-correction chains, autonomous infrastructure healing, and monotonically improving quality gates. The primitives are not new; our contribution is their composition into a production-tested system with the operational discipline that makes long-running autonomous evolution safe.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [388] [Fusion Learning from Dynamic Functional Connectivity: Combining the Amplitude and Phase of fMRI Signals to Identify Brain Disorders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24603)
*Jinlong Hu,Jiatong Huang,Zijian Cai*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: This paper introduces MSFL, a model that combines amplitude correlations and phase coherence from fMRI signals to better detect brain disorders like autism and depression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the detection of brain disorders by enhancing the dynamic functional connectivity (dFC) analysis leveraging both amplitude and phase information.

Method: The authors propose a multi-scale fusion learning framework, MSFL, which integrates sliding window correlation (SWC) and phase synchronization (PS) features from fMRI signals.

Result: The MSFL model outperformed existing comparative models in classifying autism spectrum disorder and major depressive disorder using ABIDE I and REST-meta-MDD datasets.

Conclusion: Incorporating both amplitude and phase information in dFC analysis enhances the detection of brain disorders, as validated by the superior performance of the MSFL framework and explanation analysis showing the importance of both SWC and PS features.

Abstract: Dynamic functional connectivity (dFC) derived from resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has been extensively utilized in brain science research. The sliding window correlation (SWC) method is a widely used approach for constructing dFC by computing correlation coefficients between amplitude time series of signals from pairs of brain regions. In this study, we propose an integrated approach that incorporates both amplitude and phase information of fMRI signals to improve the detection of brain disorders. Specifically, we introduce a multi-scale fusion learning framework, namely MSFL, which leverages two complementary dFC features derived from SWC and phase synchronization (PS). Here, SWC captures amplitude correlations, while PS measures phase coherence within dFC. We evaluated the efficacy of MSFL in classifying autism spectrum disorder and major depressive disorder using two publicly available datasets: ABIDE I and REST-meta-MDD, respectively. The results indicate that MSFL significantly outperforms existing comparative models. Moreover, we performed model explanation analysis using the SHAP framework, which showed that both types of dFC features from SWC and PS contribute to detecting brain disorders.

</details>


### [389] [Quantifying plasticity: a network-based framework linking structure to dynamical regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25180)
*Igor Branchi*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper formalizes the concept of plasticity in complex systems, introducing it as a predictive measure based on system size and connectivity, and links it to criticality for adaptive efficacy across disciplines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a predictive and quantifiable framework for understanding plasticity in complex systems, moving beyond retrospective descriptive concepts and linking it to criticality and system resilience.

Method: A network-based operationalization of plasticity is proposed, defining it as the ratio between system size and connectivity strength, and examining its implications for system dynamics and transitions.

Result: The study finds that optimal plasticity occurs at intermediate connectivity strength, coinciding with criticality, and demonstrates its utility in anticipating transitions in diverse systems including psychopathology.

Conclusion: Plasticity is reframed as a causal driver of criticality, serving as a system-level regulator that explains and predicts dynamic transitions, with applications across multiple fields of complexity science.

Abstract: Plasticity is a fundamental property of complex systems, such as the brain or an organism. Yet it typically remains a descriptive concept inferred retrospectively from observed outcomes, such as modifications in activity or morphology. Here, the network-based operationalization of plasticity is further formalized as the ratio between system size and connectivity strength among system elements. Within this framework, system size determines the dimensionality of the accessible state space, while connectivity strength tunes the system's regime. An optimal range of plasticity -- balancing capacity for change and capacity to maintain coherence -- emerges at intermediate connectivity strength. Notably, this balance coincides with the critical regime, which provides a theoretically motivated benchmark that enables a normalized unit of measure, termed effective plasticity, and comparisons of adaptive efficacy across diverse systems. Plasticity is thus transformed into a predictive tool that quantifies a system's capacity for change before it occurs. Its validity is supported across disciplines and, in particular, by evidence from psychopathology where it anticipates transitions between mental states. At a mechanistic level, plasticity acts as a structural tuning parameter for criticality, reframing their relationship as causal, with plasticity driving criticality rather than merely accompanying it. Furthermore, this network-based operationalization explains how larger systems can more robustly maintain critical dynamics. Crucially, the proposed perspective distinguishes functional regime shifts from thermodynamic phase changes, identifying plasticity as the system-level regulator that shapes and constrains the dynamic repertoire. This framework is applicable across domains, including ecology, economics, and social systems, and may foster cross-disciplinary integration within complexity science.

</details>


### [390] [The Reward Function and the Least Cost Principle for Gravitation and other Laws of Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25444)
*Rubén Moreno-Bote*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper explores the use of inverse reinforcement learning to infer a reward function that could explain the observed laws of motion, particularly focusing on classical gravitational and Coulomb forces, and suggests that the dynamics favor relative motion and quasi-circular orbits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand whether the laws of the universe can be interpreted as optimizing a certain cost or reward function, thereby providing insight into the underlying principles guiding natural forces.

Method: The authors introduced the 'least cost principle' and used concepts from inverse optimal control to derive the functional form of acceleration cost and infer reward functions from classical forces, such as gravitational and Coulomb laws.

Result: The inferred reward function highlights scenarios where particles have high relative velocities and orthogonal motion to distance vectors. This suggests optimization of these characteristics in natural central forces.

Conclusion: The study concludes that quasi-circular orbits and specific relative motion features are dynamical and static optimizations inherently encoded in nature's central forces.

Abstract: If the universe follows a specific design, then a central question is which cost function is optimized by the observed forces. This is the problem of inverse optimal control, or inverse reinforcement learning, in which a reward function is inferred from the dynamics of the observed system. We first establish the {\em least cost principle}, whereby the laws of motion can be derived from minimization of a time-discounted integral of the acceleration cost minus a state-dependent reward function. After determining the functional form of the acceleration cost from basic principles, we infer the reward function from the laws of motion governing classical gravitation and Coulomb forces. The inferred reward function is high when pairs of particles have high relative velocities and when their relative motion is orthogonal to their distance vectors. All in all, our work suggests that relative motion and quasi-circular orbits are the dynamical and static features optimized by central forces in nature.

</details>


### [391] [Compiling molecular ultrastructure into neural dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25713)
*Konrad P. Kording,Anton Arkhipov,Davy Deng,Sean Escola,Seth G. N. Grant,Gal Haspel,Michał Januszewski,Narayanan Kasthuri,Nina Khera,Richie E. Kohman,Grace Lindsay,Jeantine Lunshof,Adam Marblestone,David A. Markowitz,Jordan Matelsky,Brett Mensh,Patrick Mineault,Andrew Payne,Joanne Peng,Xaq Pitkow,Philip Shiu,Gregor Schuhknecht,Sven Truckenbrodt,Joshua T. Vogelstein,Edward S. Boyden*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper introduces an ultrastructure-to-dynamics compiler which transforms molecular brain imaging data into physiologically relevant parameters for simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physiological parameters governing neural dynamics remain largely unexplored using ultrastructural imaging data.

Method: The authors propose a learned mapping model that uses molecularly annotated ultrastructure and paired training data from imaging and physiological experiments.

Result: Initial findings suggest that local physiological predictions are achievable directly from structural data.

Conclusion: This approach could revolutionize neural computation studies and intervention forecasting by making structural data predictive of neural dynamics.

Abstract: High-resolution brain imaging can now capture not just synapse locations but their molecular composition, with the cost of such mapping falling exponentially. Yet such ultrastructural data has so far told us little about local neuronal physiology - specifically, the parameters (e.g., synaptic efficacies, local conductances) that govern neural dynamics. We propose to translate molecularly annotated ultrastructure into physiology, introducing the concept of an ultrastructure-to-dynamics compiler: a learned mapping from molecularly annotated ultrastructure to simulator-ready, uncertainty-aware physiological parameters. The requirement is paired training data, with jointly acquired ultrastructure from imaging, and dynamical responses to perturbations from physiological experiments. With this data we can train models that predict local physiology directly from structure. Such a compiler would support biophysical simulations by turning anatomical maps into models of circuit dynamics, shifting structure-to-function from a descriptive program to a predictive one and opening routes to understanding neural computation and forecasting intervention effects.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [392] [Improving Infinitely Deep Bayesian Neural Networks with Nesterov's Accelerated Gradient Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25024)
*Chenxu Yu,Wenqi Fang*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes an enhanced stochastic differential equation (SDE)-based Bayesian neural network model using Nesterov-accelerated gradient (NAG) and residual skip connections to reduce computational cost and improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SDE-based Bayesian neural networks, while theoretically strong, are limited by high computational costs and convergence instability due to their reliance on numerical SDE solvers.

Method: The authors integrate Nesterov-accelerated gradient into the SDE-BNN framework combined with an NFE-dependent residual skip connection to accelerate convergence and reduce numerical function evaluations (NFEs).

Result: The enhanced model demonstrates superior performance in tasks such as image classification and sequence modeling, reducing NFEs and achieving better predictive accuracy compared to conventional SDE-BNNs.

Conclusion: The proposed NAG-enhanced SDE-BNN model improves efficiency and accuracy, addressing key limitations of existing SDE-BNN methods and showing promise for broader applications.

Abstract: As a representative continuous-depth neural network approach, stochastic differential equation (SDE)-based Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) have attracted considerable attention due to their solid theoretical foundations and strong potential for real-world applications. However, their reliance on numerical SDE solvers inevitably incurs a large number of function evaluations (NFEs), resulting in high computational cost and occasional convergence instability. To address these challenges, we propose a Nesterov-accelerated gradient (NAG) enhanced SDE-BNN model. By integrating NAG into the SDE-BNN framework along with an NFE-dependent residual skip connection, our method accelerates convergence and substantially reduces NFEs during both training and testing. Extensive empirical results show that our model consistently outperforms conventional SDE-BNNs across various tasks, including image classification and sequence modeling, achieving lower NFEs and improved predictive accuracy.

</details>


### [393] [Fair regression under localized demographic parity constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25224)
*Arthur Charpentier,Christophe Denis,Romuald Elie,Mohamed Hebiri,François HU*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a relaxed group fairness criterion for regression problems called (${ll}$, Z)-fair predictor, which ensures demographic parity at selected quantiles or thresholds. This balances fairness and accuracy effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard demographic parity is too restrictive in regression settings, often leading to significant reductions in accuracy. The paper aims to relax this criterion to mitigate this negative impact while maintaining fairness.

Method: The approach involves enforcing demographic parity at finite quantile levels or score thresholds using a (${ll}$, Z)-fair predictor. The paper also develops a post-processing algorithm with sample-based calibration to achieve fairness constraints, providing theoretical guarantees for performance.

Result: Closed-form solutions for the fairness constraints are derived, revealing that the discretization cost diminishes with finer grids. Experiments show that the proposed method achieves interpretable fairness while preserving accuracy.

Conclusion: A relaxed fairness approach tailored to regression enables targeted adjustments without compromising predictive performance, offering a practical trade-off between accuracy and fairness.

Abstract: Demographic parity (DP) is a widely used group fairness criterion requiring predictive distributions to be invariant across sensitive groups. While natural in classification, full distributional DP is often overly restrictive in regression and can lead to substantial accuracy loss. We propose a relaxation of DP tailored to regression, enforcing parity only at a finite set of quantile levels and/or score thresholds. Concretely, we introduce a novel (${\ell}$, Z)-fair predictor, which imposes groupwise CDF constraints of the form F f |S=s (z m ) = ${\ell}$ m for prescribed pairs (${\ell}$ m , z m ). For this setting, we derive closed-form characterizations of the optimal fair discretized predictor via a Lagrangian dual formulation and quantify the discretization cost, showing that the risk gap to the continuous optimum vanishes as the grid is refined. We further develop a model-agnostic post-processing algorithm based on two samples (labeled for learning a base regressor and unlabeled for calibration), and establish finite-sample guarantees on constraint violation and excess penalized risk. In addition, we introduce two alternative frameworks where we match group and marginal CDF values at selected score thresholds. In both settings, we provide closed-form solutions for the optimal fair discretized predictor. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets illustrate an interpretable fairness-accuracy trade-off, enabling targeted corrections at decision-relevant quantiles or thresholds while preserving predictive performance.

</details>


### [394] [Practical Efficient Global Optimization is No-regret](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25311)
*Jingyi Wang,Haowei Wang,Nai-Yuan Chiang,Juliane Mueller,Tucker Hartland,Cosmin G. Petra*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper examines 'Efficient Global Optimization' (EGO), commonly used in Bayesian optimization. It provides theoretical bounds for cumulative regret in the practical form of EGO with a positive nugget, verifying sublinear regret behavior and analyzing the nugget's impact with numerical support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the widespread use and empirical success of practical EGO, there is a lack of theoretical understanding of its cumulative regret bounds.

Method: The paper formulates practical EGO with the addition of a positive nugget to the Gaussian process. It establishes theoretical sublinear cumulative regret bounds for kernels like squared exponential and Matérn, and explores the nugget's influence.

Result: Practical EGO is shown to be a no-regret algorithm with sublinear cumulative regret bounds for common kernels. The nugget's impact on these bounds is theoretically analyzed.

Conclusion: The practical version of EGO demonstrates both theoretical and numerical stability, validating its use in noise-free Bayesian optimization scenarios.

Abstract: Efficient global optimization (EGO) is one of the most widely used noise-free Bayesian optimization algorithms.It comprises the Gaussian process (GP) surrogate model and expected improvement (EI) acquisition function. In practice, when EGO is applied, a scalar matrix of a small positive value (also called a nugget or jitter) is usually added to the covariance matrix of the deterministic GP to improve numerical stability. We refer to this EGO with a positive nugget as the practical EGO. Despite its wide adoption and empirical success, to date, cumulative regret bounds for practical EGO have yet to be established. In this paper, we present for the first time the cumulative regret upper bound of practical EGO. In particular, we show that practical EGO has sublinear cumulative regret bounds and thus is a no-regret algorithm for commonly used kernels including the squared exponential (SE) and Matérn kernels ($ν>\frac{1}{2}$). Moreover, we analyze the effect of the nugget on the regret bound and discuss the theoretical implication on its choice. Numerical experiments are conducted to support and validate our findings.

</details>


### [395] [A Distribution-to-Distribution Neural Probabilistic Forecasting Framework for Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25370)
*Tianlin Yang,Hailiang Du,Louis Aslett*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a D2D neural framework for probabilistic forecasting, directly evolving predictive distributions without relying on ensemble or sampling methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing forecasting methods, whether based on physics or neural networks, are trajectory-oriented and depend on ensemble or sampling methods to approximate predictive distributions. This approach limits a principled representation of uncertainty in dynamical systems.

Method: The authors introduce a D2D framework that encodes and decodes predictive distributions using kernel mean embeddings and mixture density networks, replacing ensemble methods. The framework operates recursively in a unified end-to-end neural architecture.

Result: Applied to the Lorenz63 chaotic system, the D2D framework effectively captures complex distributional evolution under nonlinear dynamics and produces skillful forecasts, often outperforming a simplified perfect model benchmark.

Conclusion: The D2D framework marks a shift in probabilistic forecasting, by learning and evolving distributions directly, offering an innovative and competitive alternative to traditional ensemble-based methods.

Abstract: Probabilistic forecasting provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification in dynamical systems by representing predictions as probability distributions rather than deterministic trajectories. However, existing forecasting approaches, whether physics-based or neural-network-based, remain fundamentally trajectory-oriented: predictive distributions are usually accessed through ensembles or sampling, rather than evolved directly as dynamical objects. A distribution-to-distribution (D2D) neural probabilistic forecasting framework is developed to operate directly on predictive distributions. The framework introduces a distributional encoding and decoding structure around a replaceable neural forecasting module, using kernel mean embeddings to represent input distributions and mixture density networks to parameterise output predictive distributions. This design enables recursive propagation of predictive uncertainty within a unified end-to-end neural architecture, with model training and evaluation carried out directly in terms of probabilistic forecast skill. The framework is demonstrated on the Lorenz63 chaotic dynamical system. Results show that the D2D model captures nontrivial distributional evolution under nonlinear dynamics, produces skillful probabilistic forecasts without explicit ensemble simulation, and remains competitive with, and in some cases outperforms, a simplified perfect model benchmark. These findings point to a new paradigm for probabilistic forecasting, in which predictive distributions are learned and evolved directly rather than reconstructed indirectly through ensemble-based uncertainty propagation.

</details>


### [396] [Residual-as-Teacher: Mitigating Bias Propagation in Student--Teacher Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25466)
*Kakei Yamamoto,Martin J. Wainwright*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper examines a new method called Residual-as-Teacher (RaT) for student–teacher models, which reduces bias transferred from the teacher model to the student and achieves better statistical performance than standard approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the issue where the standard student soft matching (SM) approach propagates systematic bias from the teacher model to the student, reducing the quality of the student model's predictions.

Method: The proposed Residual-as-Teacher (RaT) method uses the teacher model to estimate residuals in the student's predictions. The method connects to proximal gradient schemes for oracle optimization problems and is analyzed theoretically for performance guarantees.

Result: The analysis shows reduced teacher bias and provides non-asymptotic excess risk bounds and convergence guarantees for RaT. For kernel-based models, RaT achieves the minimax-optimal rate, outperforming SM which suffers constant prediction errors. Experiments confirm these theoretical results.

Conclusion: RaT is a better alternative to SM for student-teacher model training, as it mitigates teacher bias and demonstrates superior empirical and theoretical performance.

Abstract: We study statistical estimation in a student--teacher setting, where predictions from a pre-trained teacher are used to guide a student model. A standard approach is to train the student to directly match the teacher's outputs, which we refer to as student soft matching (SM). This approach directly propagates any systematic bias or mis-specification present in the teacher, thereby degrading the student's predictions. We propose and analyze an alternative scheme, known as residual-as-teacher (RaT), in which the teacher is used to estimate residuals in the student's predictions. Our analysis shows how the student can thereby emulate a proximal gradient scheme for solving an oracle optimization problem, and this provably reduces the effect of teacher bias. For general student--teacher pairs, we establish non-asymptotic excess risk bounds for any RaT fixed point, along with convergence guarantees for the student-teacher iterative scheme. For kernel-based student--teacher pairs, we prove a sharp separation: the RaT method achieves the minimax-optimal rate, while the SM method incurs constant prediction error for any sample size. Experiments on both synthetic data and ImageNette classification under covariate shift corroborate our theoretical findings.

</details>


### [397] [Adaptive Subspace Modeling With Functional Tucker Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25530)
*Noah Steidle,Joppe De Jonghe,Mariya Ishteva*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a functional Tucker decomposition (FTD) that embeds mode-wise continuity constraints using reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS), enhancing multidimensional data representation for tasks like classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional tensor representations can lose important continuous data characteristics due to discretization. A need exists for a method that adapts structural decomposition to continuous processes.

Method: The authors develop a functional Tucker decomposition (FTD) with RKHS to directly model continuous data modes while maintaining the multi-linear subspace structure inherent in standard tensor decomposition approaches.

Result: FTD demonstrates improved performance in domain-variant tensor classification tasks, specifically in hyperspectral imaging and multivariate time series analysis.

Conclusion: Incorporating functional adaptability into tensor decomposition via RKHS allows for more expressive and targeted subspace modeling, providing substantial classification benefits.

Abstract: Tensors provide a structured representation for multidimensional data, yet discretization can obscure important information when such data originates from continuous processes. We address this limitation by introducing a functional Tucker decomposition (FTD) that embeds mode-wise continuity constraints directly into the decomposition. The FTD employs reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS) to model continuous modes without requiring an a-priori basis, while preserving the multi-linear subspace structure of the Tucker model. Through RKHS-driven representation, the model yields adaptive and expressive factor descriptions that enable targeted modeling of subspaces. The value of this approach is demonstrated in domain-variant tensor classification. In particular, we illustrate its effectiveness with classification tasks in hyperspectral imaging and multivariate time series analysis, highlighting the benefits of combining structural decomposition with functional adaptability.

</details>


### [398] [The Rules-and-Facts Model for Simultaneous Generalization and Memorization in Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25579)
*Gabriele Farné,Fabrizio Boncoraglio,Lenka Zdeborová*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Rules-and-Facts (RAF) model to understand how neural networks balance learning rules and memorizing exceptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a theoretical framework for understanding how neural networks simultaneously generalize (learn rules) and memorize (retain exceptions).

Method: The authors propose the RAF model, where a fraction of data derives from structured rules and another fraction from random labels, then analyze the role of overparameterization, regularization, and design choices.

Result: The study quantifies how excess capacity allows memorization, while rules and mechanisms like regularization help balance generalization and memorization.

Conclusion: The RAF model offers fundamental theoretical insights into the dual capability of modern neural networks and how design choices govern this balance.

Abstract: A key capability of modern neural networks is their capacity to simultaneously learn underlying rules and memorize specific facts or exceptions. Yet, theoretical understanding of this dual capability remains limited. We introduce the Rules-and-Facts (RAF) model, a minimal solvable setting that enables precise characterization of this phenomenon by bridging two classical lines of work in the statistical physics of learning: the teacher-student framework for generalization and Gardner-style capacity analysis for memorization. In the RAF model, a fraction $1 - \varepsilon$ of training labels is generated by a structured teacher rule, while a fraction $\varepsilon$ consists of unstructured facts with random labels. We characterize when the learner can simultaneously recover the underlying rule - allowing generalization to new data - and memorize the unstructured examples. Our results quantify how overparameterization enables the simultaneous realization of these two objectives: sufficient excess capacity supports memorization, while regularization and the choice of kernel or nonlinearity control the allocation of capacity between rule learning and memorization. The RAF model provides a theoretical foundation for understanding how modern neural networks can infer structure while storing rare or non-compressible information.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [399] [Spectral methods: crucial for machine learning, natural for quantum computers?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24654)
*Vasilis Belis,Joseph Bowles,Rishabh Gupta,Evan Peters,Maria Schuld*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper argues that quantum computing could provide new methods for machine learning, particularly through its ability to manipulate the Fourier spectrum of models in ways classical computers struggle to achieve.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how quantum computers can unlock new opportunities in machine learning by focusing on spectral methods that are essential yet computationally challenging for classical systems.

Method: The authors propose leveraging the Quantum Fourier Transform on quantum states that represent generative machine learning models, utilizing the unique capabilities of quantum computing for spectral manipulation.

Result: The paper hypothesizes that quantum computing could enable more direct and resource-efficient methods to design spectral properties of models, introducing a novel direction for quantum machine learning.

Conclusion: Quantum computing may fundamentally enhance machine learning by unlocking efficient ways to manipulate and optimize the spectral characteristics of models, thereby addressing the question of 'why quantum?' in this field.

Abstract: This article presents an argument for why quantum computers could unlock new methods for machine learning. We argue that spectral methods, in particular those that learn, regularise, or otherwise manipulate the Fourier spectrum of a machine learning model, are often natural for quantum computers. For example, if a generative machine learning model is represented by a quantum state, the Quantum Fourier Transform allows us to manipulate the Fourier spectrum of the state using the entire toolbox of quantum routines, an operation that is usually prohibitive for classical models. At the same time, spectral methods are surprisingly fundamental to machine learning: A spectral bias has recently been hypothesised to be the core principle behind the success of deep learning; support vector machines have been known for decades to regularise in Fourier space, and convolutional neural nets build filters in the Fourier space of images. Could, then, quantum computing open fundamentally different, much more direct and resource-efficient ways to design the spectral properties of a model? We discuss this potential in detail here, hoping to stimulate a direction in quantum machine learning research that puts the question of ``why quantum?'' first.

</details>


### [400] [Reinforcement learning for quantum processes with memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25138)
*Josep Lumbreras,Ruo Cheng Huang,Yanglin Hu,Marco Fanizza,Mile Gu*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper addresses reinforcement learning in quantum systems with hidden quantum dynamics by proposing an optimistic maximum-likelihood estimation algorithm, achieving optimal regret scaling and applying it to state-agnostic work extraction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the classical reinforcement learning framework to quantum systems and address the challenges posed by hidden quantum states and unknown quantum dynamics.

Method: The paper formalizes the problem with hidden quantum memory and uses quantum instruments for interaction, adapting an optimistic maximum-likelihood estimation algorithm to control error propagation and achieve optimal cumulative regret bounds.

Result: The proposed strategy achieves sublinear cumulative regret scaling as $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{K})$, with this scaling shown to be optimal up to polylogarithmic factors. The method is applied to work extraction, achieving sublinear dissipation and zero asymptotic dissipation rates.

Conclusion: The framework extends reinforcement learning to quantum systems with hidden dynamics, achieving optimal regret scaling and demonstrating practical applications in thermodynamic work extraction.

Abstract: In reinforcement learning, an agent interacts sequentially with an environment to maximize a reward, receiving only partial, probabilistic feedback. This creates a fundamental exploration-exploitation trade-off: the agent must explore to learn the hidden dynamics while exploiting this knowledge to maximize its target objective. While extensively studied classically, applying this framework to quantum systems requires dealing with hidden quantum states that evolve via unknown dynamics. We formalize this problem via a framework where the environment maintains a hidden quantum memory evolving via unknown quantum channels, and the agent intervenes sequentially using quantum instruments. For this setting, we adapt an optimistic maximum-likelihood estimation algorithm. We extend the analysis to continuous action spaces, allowing us to model general positive operator-valued measures (POVMs). By controlling the propagation of estimation errors through quantum channels and instruments, we prove that the cumulative regret of our strategy scales as $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{K})$ over $K$ episodes. Furthermore, via a reduction to the multi-armed quantum bandit problem, we establish information-theoretic lower bounds demonstrating that this sublinear scaling is strictly optimal up to polylogarithmic factors. As a physical application, we consider state-agnostic work extraction. When extracting free energy from a sequence of non-i.i.d. quantum states correlated by a hidden memory, any lack of knowledge about the source leads to thermodynamic dissipation. In our setting, the mathematical regret exactly quantifies this cumulative dissipation. Using our adaptive algorithm, the agent uses past energy outcomes to improve its extraction protocol on the fly, achieving sublinear cumulative dissipation, and, consequently, an asymptotically zero dissipation rate.

</details>


<div id='cs.CE'></div>

# cs.CE [[Back]](#toc)

### [401] [Large Language Models as Optimization Controllers: Adaptive Continuation for SIMP Topology Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25099)
*Shaoliang Yang,Jun Wang,Yunsheng Wang*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: The paper proposes an adaptive control framework using large language models (LLMs) for SIMP topology optimization, achieving improved performance over conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the effectiveness of SIMP topology optimization by replacing conventional fixed schedules with adaptive, state-aware decisions enabled by LLMs.

Method: Introduces a framework where an LLM acts as an online adaptive controller, making real-time parameter adjustments based on state observations, and integrates a meta-optimization loop for tuning agent parameters.

Result: The LLM agent outperforms four baselines in compliance outcomes on both 2D and 3D benchmarks, achieving a reduction in compliance between 5.7% and 18.1% relative to the fixed baseline.

Conclusion: LLMs effectively optimize topology by driving real-time, state-based parameter adaptation, demonstrating the potential of AI in engineering optimizations.

Abstract: We present a framework in which a large language model (LLM) acts as an online adaptive controller for SIMP topology optimization, replacing conventional fixed-schedule continuation with real-time, state-conditioned parameter decisions. At every $k$-th iteration, the LLM receives a structured observation$-$current compliance, grayness index, stagnation counter, checkerboard measure, volume fraction, and budget consumption$-$and outputs numerical values for the penalization exponent $p$, projection sharpness $β$, filter radius $r_{\min}$, and move limit $δ$ via a Direct Numeric Control interface. A hard grayness gate prevents premature binarization, and a meta-optimization loop uses a second LLM pass to tune the agent's call frequency and gate threshold across runs. We benchmark the agent against four baselines$-$fixed (no-continuation), standard three-field continuation, an expert heuristic, and a schedule-only ablation$-$on three 2-D problems (cantilever, MBB beam, L-bracket) at $120\!\times\!60$ resolution and two 3-D problems (cantilever, MBB beam) at $40\!\times\!20\!\times\!10$ resolution, all run for 300 iterations. A standardized 40-iteration sharpening tail is applied from the best valid snapshot so that compliance differences reflect only the exploration phase. The LLM agent achieves the lowest final compliance on every benchmark: $-5.7\%$ to $-18.1\%$ relative to the fixed baseline, with all solutions fully binary. The schedule-only ablation underperforms the fixed baseline on two of three problems, confirming that the LLM's real-time intervention$-$not the schedule geometry$-$drives the gain. Code and reproduction scripts will be released upon publication.

</details>


<div id='math.HO'></div>

# math.HO [[Back]](#toc)

### [402] [Shaping the Future of Mathematics in the Age of AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24914)
*Johan Commelin,Mateja Jamnik,Rodrigo Ochigame,Lenny Taelman,Akshay Venkatesh*

Main category: math.HO

TL;DR: Artificial intelligence is quickly and significantly impacting mathematics in areas such as values, practice, teaching, technology, and ethics, requiring active involvement by the math community to address its challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the rapid advancements of AI in mathematics and their implications on foundational aspects like values, practice, education, infrastructure, and ethics.

Method: The authors analyze current transformations in mathematics due to AI and propose recommendations to safeguard intellectual autonomy, reform practices, enhance curricula, build academic infrastructure, and establish ethical standards.

Result: The paper identifies critical areas influenced by AI and outlines strategies to ensure these transformations align with the interests of the mathematical community.

Conclusion: Active engagement and proactive measures are essential to preserve mathematics as shaped by its community amid AI's disruptive changes.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence is transforming mathematics at a speed and scale that demand active engagement from the mathematical community. We examine five areas where this transformation is particularly pressing: values, practice, teaching, technology, and ethics. We offer recommendations on safeguarding our intellectual autonomy, rethinking our practice, broadening curricula, building academically oriented infrastructure, and developing shared ethical principles - with the aim of ensuring that the future of mathematics is shaped by the community itself.

</details>


<div id='cs.ET'></div>

# cs.ET [[Back]](#toc)

### [403] [Characterization of Off-wafer Pulse Communication in BrainScaleS Neuromorphic System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24854)
*Bernhard Vogginger,Vasilis Thanasoulis,Johannes Partzsch,Christian Mayr*

Main category: cs.ET

TL;DR: This paper characterizes the communication infrastructure of the BrainScaleS neuromorphic system for efficient spike routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges in communication for neuromorphic systems operating 10,000x faster than biological real-time.

Method: Analyzing communication flow using metrics like throughput, delay, jitter, and pulse loss, while studying its impact on a neural benchmark model.

Result: The communication infrastructure was systematically characterized, providing insights into handling distortions and optimizing hardware mapping.

Conclusion: The study offers generalizable evaluation techniques that benefit neuromorphic systems, enhancing model-to-hardware mapping and spike channel distribution.

Abstract: Neuromorphic VLSI systems take inspiration from biology to enable efficient emulation of large-scale spiking neural networks and to explore new computational paradigms. To establish large neuromorphic systems, a sophisticated routing infrastructure is needed to communicate spikes between chips and to/from the host computer. For the BrainScaleS wafer-scale neuromorphic system considered in this work, especially the stimulation with input spikes and the recording of spikes is demanding, requiring high bandwidth and temporal resolution due to the accelerated emulation of neural dynamics 10.000 faster than biological real time. Here, we present a systematic characterization of the BrainScaleS off-wafer communication infrastructure implemented around Kintex7 FPGAs. The communication flow is characterized in terms of throughput, transmission delay, jitter and pulse loss. Further, we analyze the effect of the communication distortions (like pulse loss and jitter) on a neural benchmark model with highly varying spike activity. The presented methods and techniques for communication evaluation are general applicable and provide useful insights for the mapping of network models to the hardware such as the distribution of input spikes across communication channels.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [404] [Persistence-based topological optimization: a survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24613)
*Mathieu Carriere,Yuichi Ike,Théo Lacombe,Naoki Nishikawa*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: The paper surveys the optimization of persistence-based loss functions in computational topology, focusing on gradient descent methods, with a supporting open-source library for newcomers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically address theoretical and practical challenges in optimizing topological descriptors using gradient-based algorithms and provide a resource for newcomers.

Method: Offers a comprehensive review of persistence theory, algorithms, and applications, alongside an open-source library implementing various optimization approaches.

Result: A consolidated survey synthesizing techniques developed over the last decade and practical tools to aid understanding and experimentation.

Conclusion: The paper bridges theoretical foundations and practical applications, making the field more accessible and providing tools for further research in optimizing topological descriptors.

Abstract: Computational topology provides a tool, persistent homology, to extract quantitative descriptors from structured objects (images, graphs, point clouds, etc). These descriptors can then be involved in optimization problems, typically as a way to incorporate topological priors or to regularize machine learning models. This is usually achieved by minimizing adequate, topologically-informed losses based on these descriptors, which, in turn, naturally raises theoretical and practical questions about the possibility of optimizing such loss functions using gradient-based algorithms. This has been an active research field in the topological data analysis community over the last decade, and various techniques have been developed to enable optimization of persistence-based loss functions with gradient descent schemes. This survey presents the current state of this field, covering its theoretical foundations, the algorithmic aspects, and showcasing practical uses in several applications. It includes a detailed introduction to persistence theory and, as such, aims at being accessible to mathematicians and data scientists newcomers to the field. It is accompanied by an open-source library which implements the different approaches covered in this survey, providing a convenient playground for researchers to get familiar with the field.

</details>


<div id='physics.chem-ph'></div>

# physics.chem-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [405] [Autotuning T-PaiNN: Enabling Data-Efficient GNN Interatomic Potential Development via Classical-to-Quantum Transfer Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24752)
*Vivienne Pelletier,Vedant Bhat,Daniel J. Rivera,Steven A. Wilson,Christopher L. Muhich*

Main category: physics.chem-ph

TL;DR: This study introduces T-PaiNN, a transfer learning framework, which trains graph neural network-based interatomic potentials using classical force field data first, and then fine-tunes with smaller quantum mechanical datasets to improve data efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine-learned interatomic potentials often require large quantum datasets, which are expensive to generate, limiting their practical use.

Method: The method involves pretraining the PaiNN architecture using abundant classical force field data, followed by fine-tuning with limited density functional theory (DFT) datasets.

Result: T-PaiNN significantly reduces errors (up to 25x) in low-data cases and achieves better predictions in simulations like liquid water, saving computational costs.

Conclusion: Transfer learning using classical force fields is a powerful and efficient approach to developing accurate GNN interatomic potentials, broadening their applicability for complex systems.

Abstract: Machine-learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs), particularly graph neural network (GNN)-based models, offer a promising route to achieving near-density functional theory (DFT) accuracy at significantly reduced computational cost. However, their practical deployment is often limited by the large volumes of expensive quantum mechanical training data required. In this work, we introduce a transfer learning framework, Transfer-PaiNN (T-PaiNN), that substantially improves the data efficiency of GNN-MLIPs by leveraging inexpensive classical force field data. The approach consists of pretraining a PaiNN MLIP architecture on large-scale datasets generated from classical molecular simulations, followed by fine-tuning (dubbed autotuning) using a comparatively small DFT dataset. We demonstrate the effectiveness of autotuning T-PaiNN on both gas-phase molecular systems (QM9 dataset) and condensed-phase liquid water. Across all cases, T-PaiNN significantly outperforms models trained solely on DFT data, achieving order-of-magnitude reductions in mean absolute error while accelerating training convergence. For example, using the QM9 data set, error reductions of up to 25 times are observed in low-data regimes, while liquid water simulations show improved predictions of energies, forces, and experimentally relevant properties such as density and diffusion. These gains arise from the model's ability to learn general features of the potential energy surface from extensive classical sampling, which are subsequently refined to quantum accuracy. Overall, this work establishes transfer learning from classical force fields as a practical and computationally efficient strategy for developing high-accuracy, data-efficient GNN interatomic potentials, enabling broader application of MLIPs to complex chemical systems.

</details>


### [406] [Enabling ab initio geometry optimization of strongly correlated systems with transferable deep quantum Monte Carlo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25381)
*P. Bernát Szabó,Zeno Schätzle,Frank Noé*

Main category: physics.chem-ph

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework combining deep-learning variational Monte Carlo and Gaussian process regression for accurate and efficient exploration of molecular potential energy surfaces (PES).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of accurately exploring extended PES regions in strongly correlated systems, which is critical for understanding chemical processes.

Method: The method combines transferable deep-learning VMC to solve the electronic Schrödinger equation across molecular geometries with stochastic sampling and Gaussian process regression for sparse PES evaluation.

Result: The framework achieves zero-shot chemical accuracy for molecular geometries, enabling tasks like structure relaxation, transition-state searches, and minimum-energy pathway identification.

Conclusion: The proposed method provides an accurate way to explore complex PES landscapes, facilitating studies of bond breaking, formation, and structural rearrangements in multi-reference systems.

Abstract: A faithful description of chemical processes requires exploring extended regions of the molecular potential energy surface (PES), which remains challenging for strongly correlated systems. Transferable deep-learning variational Monte Carlo (VMC) offers a promising route by efficiently solving the electronic Schrödinger equation jointly across molecular geometries at consistently high accuracy, yet its stochastic nature renders direct exploration of molecular configuration space nontrivial. Here, we present a framework for highly accurate ab initio exploration of PESs that combines transferable deep-learning VMC with a cost-effective estimation of energies, forces, and Hessians. By continuously sampling nuclear configurations during VMC optimization of electronic wave functions, we obtain transferable descriptions that achieve zero-shot chemical accuracy within chemically relevant distributions of molecular geometries. Throughout the subsequent characterization of molecular configuration space, the PES is evaluated only sparsely, with local approximations constructed by estimating VMC energies and forces at sampled geometries and aggregating the resulting noisy data using Gaussian process regression. Our method enables accurate and efficient exploration of complex PES landscapes, including structure relaxation, transition-state searches, and minimum-energy pathways, for both ground and excited states. This opens the door to studying bond breaking, formation, and large structural rearrangements in systems with pronounced multi-reference character.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [407] [Are LLMs Overkill for Databases?: A Study on the Finiteness of SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25568)
*Yue Li,David Mimno,Unso Eun Seo Jo*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the complexity of translating natural language to SQL queries and finds that most SQL queries are highly predictable and formulaic, suggesting template methods as viable alternatives to LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to assess whether generating SQL code from natural language is inherently complex and to explore if simpler and more predictable methods, like templates, could outperform LLM-based approaches in terms of utility, safety, and cost.

Method: The authors analyzed a sample of 376 databases to understand the distribution and complexity of SQL queries, measuring factors like their template patterns and relationships to database table counts.

Result: The study found a Power Law distribution where 70% of queries can be covered by just 13% of query templates, showing that most SQL queries are predictable and not heavily reliant on database complexity.

Conclusion: LLMs for SQL code generation might be operating in a narrow, formulaic domain, making template-based solutions a potentially more efficient, safe, and cost-effective alternative.

Abstract: Translating natural language to SQL for data retrieval has become more accessible thanks to code generation LLMs. But how hard is it to generate SQL code? While databases can become unbounded in complexity, the complexity of queries is bounded by real life utility and human needs. With a sample of 376 databases, we show that SQL queries, as translations of natural language questions are finite in practical complexity. There is no clear monotonic relationship between increases in database table count and increases in complexity of SQL queries. In their template forms, SQL queries follow a Power Law-like distribution of frequency where 70% of our tested queries can be covered with just 13% of all template types, indicating that the high majority of SQL queries are predictable. This suggests that while LLMs for code generation can be useful, in the domain of database access, they may be operating in a narrow, highly formulaic space where templates could be safer, cheaper, and auditable.

</details>


<div id='cs.FL'></div>

# cs.FL [[Back]](#toc)

### [408] [Synchronous Signal Temporal Logic for Decidable Verification of Cyber-Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25531)
*Partha Roop,Sobhan Chatterjee,Avinash Malik,Nathan Allen,Logan Kenwright*

Main category: cs.FL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Synchronous Signal Temporal Logic (SSTL), a decidable fragment of Signal Temporal Logic (STL), for verifying safety-critical Cyber Physical Systems (CPS). It applies the method on a human heart model and other case studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cyber Physical Systems often operate in safety-critical environments where ensuring reliability and trustworthiness is crucial. Static verification of STL has limitations, necessitating a decidable fragment for practical use.

Method: The authors propose SSTL, which assumes signals sampled at fixed discrete steps (ticks) and introduces the Signal Invariance Hypothesis (SIH). They define SSTL's syntax and semantics and achieve decidability by translating SSTL to LTL_P and performing model checking using the SPIN tool.

Result: The study confirms that SSTL is a decidable fragment of STL and establishes the equivalence between STL and SSTL under the SIH. The method is validated through demonstration on a 33-node human heart model and other applied scenarios.

Conclusion: SSTL effectively addresses the limitations of undecidable STL in static verification. It enables decidable and rigorous safety property verification for CPS, making it practical for real-world systems.

Abstract: Many Cyber Physical System (CPS) work in a safety-critical environment, where correct execution, reliability and trustworthiness are essential. Signal Temporal Logic (STL) provides a formal framework for checking safety-critical CPS. However, static verification of STL is undecidable in general, except when we want to verify using run-time-based methods, which have limitations. We propose Synchronous Signal Temporal Logic (SSTL), a decidable fragment of STL, which admits static safety and liveness property verification. In SSTL, we assume that a signal is sampled at fixed discrete steps, called ticks, and then propose a hypothesis, called the Signal Invariance Hypothesis (SIH), which is inspired by a similar hypothesis for synchronous programs. We define the syntax and semantics of SSTL and show that SIH is a necessary and sufficient condition for equivalence between an STL formula and its SSTL counterpart. By translating SSTL to LTL_P (LTL defined over predicates), we enable decidable model checking using the SPIN model checker. We demonstrate the approach on a 33-node human heart model and other case studies.

</details>


<div id='econ.EM'></div>

# econ.EM [[Back]](#toc)

### [409] [Conformal Prediction for Nonparametric Instrumental Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25509)
*Masahiro Kato*

Main category: econ.EM

TL;DR: Introduced a method for creating distribution-free prediction intervals in NPIV regression, guaranteeing finite-sample coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of constructing prediction intervals in NPIV regression with finite-sample guarantees.

Method: Reformulated conditional coverage as marginal coverage via the conformal inference framework, adaptable to various NPIV estimators, such as sieve 2SLS and neural networks.

Result: Established theoretical guarantees for finite-sample coverage across selected IV shift classes.

Conclusion: The method is versatile and practical, offering robust predictions while accommodating a wide array of NPIV estimators.

Abstract: We propose a method for constructing distribution-free prediction intervals in nonparametric instrumental variable regression (NPIV), with finite-sample coverage guarantees. Building on the conditional guarantee framework in conformal inference, we reformulate conditional coverage as marginal coverage over a class of IV shifts $\mathcal{F}$. Our method can be combined with any NPIV estimator, including sieve 2SLS and other machine-learning-based NPIV methods such as neural networks minimax approaches. Our theoretical analysis establishes distribution-free, finite-sample coverage over a practitioner-chosen class of IV shifts.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [410] [Binary Expansion Group Intersection Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24763)
*Sicheng Zhou,Kai Zhang*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper introduces the BEGIN framework to model conditional independence for multivariate binary and multinomial data. BEGIN generalizes Gaussian graphical modeling beyond Gaussian settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conditional independence is vital in modern statistics but lacks a universal covariance characterization for non-parametric cases, motivating this work to propose BEGIN for multivariate binary and multinomial variables.

Method: The authors use a binary expansion group intersection network (BEGIN) to represent conditional independence through sparse linear representations, block factorizations of covariance matrices, and generalized Schur complements.

Result: The framework demonstrates that BEGIN constructs a graphical model for binary data based on group interactions, and approximates conditional independence for general random vectors using dyadic bit representations.

Conclusion: BEGIN provides a novel, distribution-free way to model conditional independence, paving the way for broader applications beyond Gaussian graphical models.

Abstract: Conditional independence is central to modern statistics, but beyond special parametric families it rarely admits an exact covariance characterization. We introduce the binary expansion group intersection network (BEGIN), a distribution-free graphical representation for multivariate binary data and bit-encoded multinomial variables. For arbitrary binary random vectors and bit representations of multinomial variables, we prove that conditional independence is equivalent to a sparse linear representation of conditional expectations, to a block factorization of the corresponding interaction covariance matrix, and to block diagonality of an associated generalized Schur complement. The resulting graph is indexed by the intersection of multiplicative groups of binary interactions, yielding an analogue of Gaussian graphical modeling beyond the Gaussian setting. This viewpoint treats data bits as atoms and local BEGIN molecules as building blocks for large Markov random fields. We also show how dyadic bit representations allow BEGIN to approximate conditional independence for general random vectors under mild regularity conditions. A key technical device is the Hadamard prism, a linear map that links interaction covariances to group structure.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.GN'></div>

# q-bio.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [411] [A Large-Scale Comparative Analysis of Imputation Methods for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24626)
*Yuichiro Iwashita,Ahtisham Fazeel Abbasi,Muhammad Nabeel Asim,Andreas Dengel*

Main category: q-bio.GN

TL;DR: The paper benchmarks 15 scRNA-seq imputation methods, finding traditional methods often outperform deep learning approaches and emphasizing the importance of task-specific evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the uncertainty and variability in the performance of scRNA-seq imputation methods, which suffer from dropout effects distorting gene expression data.

Method: The researchers conducted a comprehensive benchmark of 15 scRNA-seq imputation methods covering 7 categories, evaluated across 30 datasets and 6 downstream analytical tasks.

Result: Traditional methods generally outperformed modern deep learning-based approaches; performance varied significantly across datasets, protocols, and tasks, with no method dominating universally.

Conclusion: Task-specific evaluation is crucial when assessing scRNA-seq imputation methods, as different methods excel under different circumstances, and no single method can be universally recommended.

Abstract: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) is inherently affected by sparsity caused by dropout events, in which expressed genes are recorded as zeros due to technical limitations. These artifacts distort gene expression distributions and can compromise downstream analyses. Numerous imputation methods have been proposed to address this, and these methods encompass a wide range of approaches from traditional statistical models to recently developed deep learning (DL)-based methods. However, their comparative performance remains unclear, as existing benchmarking studies typically evaluate only a limited subset of methods, datasets, and downstream analytical tasks. Here, we present a comprehensive benchmark of 15 scRNA-seq imputation methods spanning 7 methodological categories, including traditional and modern DL-based methods. These methods are evaluated across 30 datasets sourced from 10 experimental protocols and assessed in terms of 6 downstream analytical tasks. Our results show that traditional imputation methods, such as model-based, smoothing-based, and low-rank matrix-based methods, generally outperform DL-based methods, such as diffusion-based, GAN-based, GNN-based, and autoencoder-based methods. In addition, strong performance in numerical gene expression recovery does not necessarily translate into improved biological interpretability in downstream analyses. Furthermore, the performance of imputation methods varies substantially across datasets, protocols, and downstream analytical tasks, and no single method consistently outperforms others across all evaluation scenarios. Together, our results provide practical guidance for selecting imputation methods tailored to specific analytical objectives and highlight the importance of task-specific evaluation when assessing imputation performance in scRNA-seq data analysis.

</details>


<div id='cs.SI'></div>

# cs.SI [[Back]](#toc)

### [412] [From Manipulation to Mistrust: Explaining Diverse Micro-Video Misinformation for Robust Debunking in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.25423)
*Zhi Zeng,Yifei Yang,Jiaying Wu,Xulang Zhang,Xiangzheng Kong,Herun Wan,Zihan Ma,Minnan Luo*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: This paper introduces WildFakeBench, a benchmark for micro-video misinformation, and FakeAgent, a framework for attribution-rich analysis of misinformation. Both aim to advance explainable detection of diverse misinformation types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid spread of misinformation via micro-videos and the limitations of current detection benchmarks and models motivated the creation of a more comprehensive multimodal benchmark and detection framework.

Method: The authors introduce WildFakeBench, a dataset of over 10,000 annotated micro-videos, and FakeAgent, a multi-agent reasoning framework that combines multimodal analysis and external evidence for fine-grained misinformation attribution.

Result: FakeAgent significantly outperforms existing models for misinformation detection across all tested categories, and WildFakeBench offers a robust testbed for research in this domain.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the effectiveness of FakeAgent and highlights the utility of WildFakeBench in driving interpretability and real-world applicability of misinformation detection models, addressing clear gaps in existing approaches.

Abstract: The rise of micro-videos has reshaped how misinformation spreads, amplifying its speed, reach, and impact on public trust. Existing benchmarks typically focus on a single deception type, overlooking the diversity of real-world cases that involve multimodal manipulation, AI-generated content, cognitive bias, and out-of-context reuse. Meanwhile, most detection models lack fine-grained attribution, limiting interpretability and practical utility. To address these gaps, we introduce WildFakeBench, a large-scale benchmark of over 10,000 real-world micro-videos covering diverse misinformation types and sources, each annotated with expert-defined attribution labels. Building on this foundation, we develop FakeAgent, a Delphi-inspired multi-agent reasoning framework that integrates multimodal understanding with external evidence for attribution-grounded analysis. FakeAgent jointly analyzes content and retrieved evidence to identify manipulation, recognize cognitive and AI-generated patterns, and detect out-of-context misinformation. Extensive experiments show that FakeAgent consistently outperforms existing MLLMs across all misinformation types, while WildFakeBench provides a realistic and challenging testbed for advancing explainable micro-video misinformation detection. Data and code are available at: https://github.com/Aiyistan/FakeAgent.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [413] [A Learnable SIM Paradigm: Fundamentals, Training Techniques, and Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24599)
*Hetong Wang,Yashuai Cao,Tiejun Lv*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIMs) offer an innovative wireless hardware capable of analog computing, with structural similarities to artificial neural networks for enhanced wireless signal processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To leverage the structural similarities between SIMs and ANN to improve wireless systems, particularly for next-gen applications such as 6G.

Method: Developing a learnable SIM architecture and applying it to machine learning paradigms for solving wireless signal separation and anti-jamming challenges.

Result: SIM-based system demonstrates enhanced spectrum utilization efficiency and anti-jamming capabilities in a lightweight design.

Conclusion: SIMs can serve as a foundation for ultra-efficient, intelligent wireless frameworks in future networks.

Abstract: Stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIMs) represent a breakthrough in wireless hardware by comprising multilayer, programmable metasurfaces capable of analog computing in the electromagnetic (EM) wave domain. By examining their architectural analogies, this article reveals a deeper connection between SIMs and artificial neural networks (ANNs). Leveraging this profound structural similarity, this work introduces a learnable SIM architecture and proposes a learnable SIM-based machine learning (ML) paradigm for sixth-generation (6G)-andbeyond systems. Then, we develop two SIM-empowered wireless signal processing schemes to effectively achieve multi-user signal separation and distinguish communication signals from jamming signals. The use cases highlight that the proposed SIM-enabled signal processing system can significantly enhance spectrum utilization efficiency and anti-jamming capability in a lightweight manner and pave the way for ultra-efficient and intelligent wireless infrastructures.

</details>


### [414] [FED-HARGPT: A Hybrid Centralized-Federated Approach of a Transformer-based Architecture for Human Context Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24601)
*Wandemberg Gibaut,Alexandre Osorio,Amparo Munoz,Sildolfo F. G. Neto,Fabio Grassiotto*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes using a hybrid centralized-federated approach with a Transformer architecture for Human Activity Recognition (HAR) to maintain privacy while achieving high performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by the need to process sensitive mobile sensor data from edge devices for tasks like monitoring human activities while ensuring data remains private.

Method: A hybrid centralized-federated learning approach using Federated Learning in the Flower framework. The federated model is tested against a centralized baseline for robustness and accuracy in a non-IID data scenario.

Result: The hybrid federated learning setup demonstrated comparable performance to centralized models, showing its effectiveness in maintaining accuracy and robustness while ensuring data privacy.

Conclusion: Federated Learning is a viable solution to balance data privacy and model performance in Human Activity Recognition applications utilizing edge devices and mobile sensor data.

Abstract: The study explores a hybrid centralized-federated approach for Human Activity Recognition (HAR) using a Transformer-based architecture. With the increasing ubiquity of edge devices, such as smartphones and wearables, a significant amount of private data from wearable and inertial sensors is generated, facilitating discreet monitoring of human activities, including resting, sleeping, and walking. This research focuses on deploying HAR technologies using mobile sensor data and leveraging Federated Learning within the Flower framework to evaluate the training of a federated model derived from a centralized baseline. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed hybrid approach in improving the accuracy and robustness of HAR models while preserving data privacy in a non-IID data scenario. The federated learning setup demonstrated comparable performance to centralized models, highlighting the potential of federated learning to strike a balance between data privacy and model performance in real-world applications.

</details>


### [415] [MuViS: Multimodal Virtual Sensing Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.24602)
*Jens U. Brandt,Noah C. Puetz,Jobel Jose George,Niharika Vinay Kumar,Elena Raponi,Marc Hilbert,Thomas Bäck,Thomas Bartz-Beielstein*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces MuViS, an open-source benchmarking suite aimed at standardizing and evaluating multimodal virtual sensing approaches across diverse datasets and sensing configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the fragmented research landscape in virtual sensing and establish a domain-agnostic tool for standardized evaluation across modalities, datasets, and sensing configurations.

Method: Developed MuViS as a benchmarking suite that integrates diverse datasets into a unified interface for preprocessing and evaluation. Benchmarked various models, including gradient-boosted decision trees and deep neural networks.

Result: The benchmarking revealed no single model excels universally across tasks, highlighting the need for more generalizable virtual sensing architectures.

Conclusion: MuViS serves as a key infrastructure for developing and evaluating virtual sensing models, offering extensibility and reproducibility, and emphasizing the need for universal solutions in the field.
